title.zh: 卷五十三 景十三王傳
title.en: Volume 53: The thirteen sons of Emperor Jing
title.raw: 卷五十三 景十三王傳 Volume 53: The thirteen sons of Emperor Jing

sentences.zh: 卷五十三 景十三王傳第二十三

translations.literal: Volume 53: Biography of the Thirteen Sons of Emperor Jing, Number Twenty-three
translations.idiomatic: Volume 53: Biography of Emperor Jing’s Thirteen Sons (no. 23)

sentences.zh: 孝景皇帝十四男。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaojing had fourteen sons.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing fathered fourteen sons.

sentences.zh: 王皇后生孝武皇帝。

translations.literal: Empress Wang bore Emperor Xiaowu.
translations.idiomatic: The Empress Wang gave birth to the future Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 栗姬生臨江閔王榮、河間獻王德、臨江哀王閼。

translations.literal: Consort Li bore Prince Min of Linjiang Rong, Prince Xian of Hejian De, and Prince Ai of Linjiang Que.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Li was mother to Liu Rong, Prince Min of Linjiang; Liu De, Prince Xian of Hejian; and Liu Que, Prince Ai of Linjiang.

sentences.zh: 程姬生魯共王餘、江都易王非、膠西于王端。

translations.literal: Consort Cheng bore Prince Gong of Lu Yu, Prince Yi of Jiangdu Fei, and Prince Yu of Jiaoxi Duan.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Cheng bore Liu Yu, Prince Gong of Lu; Liu Fei, Prince Yi of Jiangdu; and Liu Duan, Prince Yu of Jiaoxi.

sentences.zh: 賈夫人生趙敬肅王彭祖、中山靖王勝。

translations.literal: Lady Jia bore Prince Jingsu of Zhao Pengzu and Prince Jing of Zhongshan Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Consort Jia was mother to Liu Pengzu, Prince Jingsu of Zhao, and Liu Sheng, Prince Jing of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 唐姬生長沙定王發。

translations.literal: Lady Tang bore Prince Ding of Changsha Fa.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Tang bore Liu Fa, Prince Ding of Changsha.

sentences.zh: 王夫人生廣川惠王越、膠東康王寄、清河哀王乘、常山憲王舜。

translations.literal: Lady Wang bore Prince Hui of Guangchuan Yue, Prince Kang of Jiaodong Ji, Prince Ai of Qinghe Cheng, and Prince Xian of Changshan Shun.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Wang bore Liu Yue, Prince Hui of Guangchuan; Liu Ji, Prince Kang of Jiaodong; Liu Cheng, Prince Ai of Qinghe; and Liu Shun, Prince Xian of Changshan.

sentences.zh: 河間獻王德

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Hejian, De
translations.idiomatic: Liu De, Prince Xian of Hejian

sentences.zh: 河間獻王德以孝景前二年立，修學好古，實事求是。

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Hejian De was established in the second year of Emperor Xiaojing’s reign before his accession; he cultivated learning and loved antiquity, seeking truth in concrete facts.
translations.idiomatic: Liu De, Prince Xian of Hejian, was ennobled in the second year of Emperor Jing’s pre-accession era. He devoted himself to scholarship and antiquity, and held to the principle of “seeking truth in solid evidence.”

sentences.zh: 從民得善書，必為好寫與之，留其真，加金帛賜以招之。

translations.literal: When he obtained fine books from the people, he always had them carefully copied and returned the originals, adding gifts of gold and silk to attract more.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever commoners brought him valuable texts, he had fair copies made for them, returned their originals, and rewarded them with gold and silk to draw in still more books.

sentences.zh: 繇是四方道術之人不遠千里，或有先祖舊書，多奉以奏獻王者，故得書多，與漢朝等。

translations.literal: Thereupon men of the Way from the four quarters, not shrinking from a journey of a thousand li, and some who had old books handed down from ancestors, mostly presented them to the prince; therefore he acquired as many books as the Han court itself.
translations.idiomatic: Scholars and adepts flocked from every quarter, some bearing family heirlooms in old script; many offered them to the prince, so that his library grew as large as that of the imperial Han government.

sentences.zh: 是時，淮南王安亦好書，所招致率多浮辯。

translations.literal: At that time Prince Huainan An also loved books, but those he gathered were mostly empty rhetoric.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Liu An of Huainan likewise collected books, yet the hangers-on he attracted dealt chiefly in flashy sophistry.

sentences.zh: 獻王所得書皆古文先秦舊書，周官、尚書、禮、禮記、孟子、老子之屬，皆經傳說記，七十子之徒所論。

translations.literal: The books Prince Xian obtained were all ancient-script texts from before the Qin—the Zhou offices, Documents, Ritual, Record of Ritual, Mencius, Laozi, and the like—all classics, commentaries, and discourses discussed by the disciples of the seventy masters.
translations.idiomatic: What Liu De acquired were archaic pre-Qin manuscripts: the Rites of Zhou, the Book of Documents, the Rituals, the Record of Rites, the Mencius, the Laozi, and similar works—canonical texts, glosses, and treatises associated with the seventy followers of Confucius.

sentences.zh: 其學舉六藝，立毛氏詩、左氏春秋博士。

translations.literal: His learning embraced the six arts; he established chairs for the Mao version of the Odes and the Zuo commentary on the Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: His curriculum covered the six arts, and he appointed erudits for the Mao tradition of the Odes and the Zuo commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals.

sentences.zh: 修禮樂，被服儒術，造次必於儒者。

translations.literal: He cultivated ritual and music, clothed himself in Confucian teaching, and in every haste acted as a Confucian would.
translations.idiomatic: He cultivated ritual and music, wore the mantle of Confucian learning, and even in moments of urgency behaved as a scholar ought.

sentences.zh: 山東諸儒者從而游。

translations.literal: Confucians east of the mountains followed him on his travels.
translations.idiomatic: Ru scholars from east of the mountains came to study at his court.

sentences.zh: 武帝時，獻王來朝，獻雅樂，對三雍宮及詔策所問三十餘事。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Wu, when Prince Xian came to court he presented court music and answered more than thirty matters raised in the three Yong palaces and in edicts and interrogatories.
translations.idiomatic: During Emperor Wu’s reign Liu De visited the capital, presented refined ritual music, and responded to over thirty questions put to him in the Three Yong halls and in imperial rescripts.

sentences.zh: 其對推道術而言，得事之中，文約指明。

translations.literal: His replies expounded the Way and techniques of government, hit the mean of affairs, were concise in wording and clear in pointing.
translations.idiomatic: His answers unfolded statecraft and principle, struck the right balance on each issue, and were spare in diction yet precise in judgment.

sentences.zh: 立二十六年薨。

translations.literal: After twenty-six years of rule he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the twenty-sixth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 中尉常麗以聞，曰：「王身端行治，溫仁恭儉，篤敬愛下，明知深察，惠于鰥寡。」

translations.literal: The commandant of the capital guard Chang Li reported it, saying: “The king’s person is upright and his conduct well ordered; he is warm, humane, respectful, and frugal; he is sincere in reverence and loving to those below; his understanding is clear and his insight deep; he shows kindness to widowers and widows.”
translations.idiomatic: Commandant of the capital guard Chang Li memorialized: “The king’s person and conduct are exemplary—mild, humane, reverent, and thrifty; he honors superiors and cherishes inferiors; his judgment is lucid and penetrating; he shows compassion to the widowed and alone.”

sentences.zh: 大行令奏：「諡法曰『聰明睿知曰獻』，宜諡曰獻王。」

translations.literal: The grand coachman memorialized: “The canons of posthumous names say ‘wise and perspicacious is called Xian’; he should receive the posthumous title Prince Xian.”
translations.idiomatic: The Minister of Imperial Equipage reported: “By the rules of ennobling the dead, ‘keen of mind and far-seeing’ is styled Xian; the late king merits the posthumous title Prince Xian.”

sentences.zh: 子共王不害嗣，四年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Gong Buhai succeeded; after four years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Buhai, Prince Gong, succeeded and died four years later.

sentences.zh: 子剛王堪嗣，十二年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Gang Kan succeeded; after twelve years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Kan, Prince Gang, reigned twelve years and then died.

sentences.zh: 子頃王授嗣，十七年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Qing Shou succeeded; after seventeen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Shou, Prince Qing, succeeded and died in the seventeenth year.

sentences.zh: 子孝王慶嗣，四十三年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Xiao Qing succeeded; after forty-three years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Qing, Prince Xiao, held the fief for forty-three years before his death.

sentences.zh: 子元嗣。

translations.literal: His son Yuan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yuan succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 元取故廣陵厲王、厲王太子及中山懷王故姬廉等以為姬。

translations.literal: Yuan took as concubines Lian and others who had been concubines of the late Prince Li of Guangling, the late crown prince of Li, and Prince Huai of Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yuan took into his harem Lian and other former consorts of the deposed Prince Li of Guangling, that prince’s former heir, and Prince Huai of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 甘露中，冀州刺史敞奏元，事下廷尉，逮召廉等。

translations.literal: In the Ganlu era, the inspector of Ji province Chang memorialized against Yuan; the matter went down to the commandant of justice, who summoned Lian and the others.
translations.idiomatic: During the Ganlu period, Ji provincial inspector Chang denounced Liu Yuan; the case was referred to the commandant of justice, and Lian and her companions were summoned.

sentences.zh: 元迫脅凡七人，令自殺。

translations.literal: Yuan coerced seven persons in all and ordered them to kill themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan forced seven people in all to take their own lives under duress.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請誅元，有詔削二縣，萬一千戶。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized asking that Yuan be executed; an edict ordered two counties and eleven thousand households struck from his fief.
translations.idiomatic: Officials asked for Yuan’s death; the emperor instead ordered two counties and eleven thousand households removed from his domain.

sentences.zh: 後元怒少史留貴，留貴踰垣出，欲告元，元使人殺留貴母。

translations.literal: Later Yuan was angry with his junior clerk Liu Gui; Liu Gui climbed over the wall to flee and intended to denounce Yuan, so Yuan had Liu Gui’s mother killed.
translations.idiomatic: Later Yuan quarreled with his junior scribe Liu Gui; Liu Gui scaled the wall to escape and meant to inform on him, so Yuan had Liu Gui’s mother murdered.

sentences.zh: 有司奏元殘賊不改，不可君國子民。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized that Yuan was cruel and vicious without reform and could not rule a state or be father to the people.
translations.idiomatic: Officials reported that Yuan remained savage and incorrigible and was unfit to hold a kingdom or act as lord to his subjects.

sentences.zh: 廢勿王，處漢中房陵。

translations.literal: He was deposed from kingship and not allowed to be king, and was settled at Fangling in Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He was stripped of his royal title and confined to Fangling in Hanzhong commandery.

sentences.zh: 居數年，坐與妻若共乘朱輪車，怒若，又笞擊，令自髡。

translations.literal: After several years, for sitting in a vermilion-wheeled carriage with his wife Ruo and then, angry with Ruo, beating her and ordering her to shave her own head.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later he was charged because he rode in a vermilion-wheeled carriage with his consort Ruo, flew into a rage at her, struck her, and made her shave her own head.

sentences.zh: 漢中太守請治元，病死。

translations.literal: The governor of Hanzhong asked permission to try Yuan; he died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: The Hanzhong governor sought authority to prosecute him; Yuan died of illness first.

sentences.zh: 立十七年，國除。

translations.literal: In the seventeenth year of his establishment the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: After seventeen years on the throne his domain was extinguished.

sentences.zh: 絕五歲，成帝建始元年，復立元弟上郡庫令良，是為河間惠王。

translations.literal: After five years of extinction, in the first year of Jianshi of Emperor Cheng, Yuan’s younger brother Liang, keeper of the arsenal at Shangjun, was re-established as king—this was Prince Hui of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: The line lapsed for five years until, in Emperor Cheng’s first Jianshi year, Yuan’s younger brother Liang, arsenal warden at Shangjun, was restored as Prince Hui of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 良修獻王之行，母太后薨，服喪如禮。

translations.literal: Liang cultivated Prince Xian’s conduct; when the queen dowager his mother died, he observed mourning according to ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Liang emulated Prince Xian’s virtue; when his mother the queen dowager died, he observed the full mourning rites.

sentences.zh: 哀帝下詔褒揚曰：「河間王良，喪太后三年，為宗室儀表，其益封萬戶。」

translations.literal: Emperor Ai issued an edict praising him: “Prince Liang of Hejian observed three years’ mourning for the queen dowager; he is a model for the imperial clan; increase his fief by ten thousand households.”
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ai issued a rescript of praise: “Liu Liang of Hejian mourned the queen dowager the full three years; as an exemplar for the house of Liu, add ten thousand households to his estate.”

sentences.zh: 二十七年薨。

translations.literal: After twenty-seven years he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the twenty-seventh year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子尚嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Shang succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Shang succeeded; the house died out under Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 臨江哀王閼

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Linjiang, Que
translations.idiomatic: Liu Que, Prince Ai of Linjiang

sentences.zh: 臨江哀王閼以孝景前二年立，三年薨。

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Linjiang Que was established in the second year before Emperor Jing’s accession; after three years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Que, Prince Ai of Linjiang, was ennobled in Emperor Jing’s second pre-accession year and died three years later.

sentences.zh: 無子，國除為郡。

translations.literal: Having no son, the state was abolished and became a commandery.
translations.idiomatic: With no heir, his kingdom was struck off and converted into a commandery.

sentences.zh: 臨江閔王榮

translations.literal: Prince Min of Linjiang, Rong
translations.idiomatic: Liu Rong, Prince Min of Linjiang

sentences.zh: 臨江閔王榮以孝景前四年為皇太子，四歲廢為臨江王。

translations.literal: Prince Min of Linjiang Rong was made heir apparent in the fourth year before Emperor Jing’s accession; after four years he was deposed and made Prince of Linjiang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Rong was appointed crown prince in Emperor Jing’s fourth pre-accession year; four years later he was removed and enfeoffed as Prince of Linjiang.

sentences.zh: 三歲，坐侵廟壖地為宮，上徵榮。

translations.literal: In the third year, for encroaching on the ritual precincts of the temple to build a palace, the emperor summoned Rong.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of that princedom he was charged with building a palace on land reserved for the ancestral temple; the emperor summoned Rong to the capital.

sentences.zh: 榮行，祖於江陵北門，既上車，軸折車廢。

translations.literal: When Rong set out, they performed the road sacrifice at the north gate of Jiangling; he had already mounted the carriage when the axle broke and the carriage was ruined.
translations.idiomatic: As he departed, a farewell sacrifice was offered at Jiangling’s north gate; once he had boarded the chariot, its axle snapped and the vehicle broke down.

sentences.zh: 江陵父老流涕竊言曰：「吾王不反矣！」

translations.literal: The elders of Jiangling wept in tears and whispered among themselves: “Our king will not return!”
translations.idiomatic: The old men of Jiangling wept and murmured among themselves, “Our prince will never come back.”

sentences.zh: 榮至，詣中尉府對簿。

translations.literal: When Rong arrived, he went to the commandant of the capital guard’s office to answer the indictment.
translations.idiomatic: On reaching the capital he presented himself at the commandant of the capital guard’s yamen to face the bill of accusation.

sentences.zh: 中尉郅都簿責訊王，王恐，自殺。

translations.literal: The commandant Zhi Du examined and interrogated the king in the register; the king, in fear, killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Commandant Zhi Du browbeat him through the written record; terrified, Rong took his own life.

sentences.zh: 葬藍田，燕數萬銜土置冢上。

translations.literal: He was buried at Lantian; swallows in the tens of thousands carried mud and placed it on his mound.
translations.idiomatic: He was interred at Lantian, where tens of thousands of swallows brought earth and piled it upon his tomb.

sentences.zh: 百姓憐之。

translations.literal: The common people pitied him.
translations.idiomatic: The people mourned him.

sentences.zh: 榮最長，亡子，國除。

translations.literal: Rong was the eldest; he had no son; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Rong was the eldest son; he left no heir, and his kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 地入于漢，為南郡。

translations.literal: The territory passed into Han and became Nan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His lands reverted to the Han and were organized as Nan commandery.

sentences.zh: 魯恭王餘

translations.literal: Prince Gong of Lu, Yu
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yu, Prince Gong of Lu

sentences.zh: 魯恭王餘以孝景前二年立為淮陽王。

translations.literal: Prince Gong of Lu Yu was established as Prince of Huaiyang in the second year before Emperor Jing’s accession.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yu, Prince Gong of Lu, was first made Prince of Huaiyang in Emperor Jing’s second pre-accession year.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反破後，以孝景前三年徙王魯。

translations.literal: After the defeat of the Wu and Chu rebellion, in the third year before Emperor Jing’s accession he was transferred to be king of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Once the Wu–Chu revolt had been crushed, in the third pre-accession year he was moved to the throne of Lu.

sentences.zh: 好治宮室苑囿狗馬，季年好音，不喜辭。

translations.literal: He loved to build palaces, parks, kennels, and stables; in his later years he loved music and did not delight in rhetoric.
translations.idiomatic: He delighted in palaces, hunting parks, dogs, and horses; in old age he turned to music and cared little for literary display.

sentences.zh: 為人口吃難言。

translations.literal: In person he stammered and found speech difficult.
translations.idiomatic: He was a stutterer and spoke with difficulty.

sentences.zh: 二十八年薨。

translations.literal: After twenty-eight years he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the twenty-eighth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子安王光嗣，初好音樂輿馬，晚節遴，唯恐不足於財。

translations.literal: His son Prince An Guang succeeded; at first he loved music and carriages, but in his later years he became miserly and feared only that he would lack wealth.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Guang, Prince An, succeeded; in youth he loved music and equipage, but in later life he grew stingy and fretted constantly over money.

sentences.zh: 四十年薨。

translations.literal: After forty years he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died after forty years on the throne.

sentences.zh: 子孝王慶忌嗣，三十七年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Xiao Qingji succeeded; after thirty-seven years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Qingji, Prince Xiao, reigned thirty-seven years and died.

sentences.zh: 子頃王勁嗣，二十八年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Qing Jin succeeded; after twenty-eight years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Jin, Prince Qing, held the fief twenty-eight years before his death.

sentences.zh: 子文王晳嗣，十八年薨，亡子，國除。

translations.literal: His son Prince Wen Xi succeeded; after eighteen years he died, having no son; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Xi, Prince Wen, died in the eighteenth year without issue, and the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 哀帝建平三年，復立頃王子晳弟郚鄉侯閔為王。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianping of Emperor Ai, Xi’s younger brother Min, marquis of Xiang township, was re-established as king.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Ai’s third Jianping year, Xi’s younger brother Min, Marquis of Xiang township, was restored as prince.

sentences.zh: 王莽時絕。

translations.literal: Under Wang Mang the line ended.
translations.idiomatic: The line was cut off under Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 恭王初好治宮室，壞孔子舊宅以廣其宮，聞鐘磬琴瑟之聲，遂不敢復壞，於其壁中得古文經傳。

translations.literal: At first Prince Gong loved to build palaces; he demolished Confucius’s old house to enlarge his palace, but hearing the sound of bells, chimes, zithers, and lutes, he dared not demolish further, and in its walls he obtained the ancient-script classics and commentaries.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Gong Yu first razed part of Confucius’s former dwelling to expand his palace; when he heard bells, stone-chimes, and strings from within, he stopped the work—and from the walls workers recovered classical texts in old script.

sentences.zh: 江都易王非

translations.literal: Prince Yi of Jiangdu, Fei
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fei, Prince Yi of Jiangdu

sentences.zh: 國中口語籍籍，慎無復至江都。」

translations.literal: In the state rumors spread thickly; by no means return again to Jiangdu.”
translations.idiomatic: …“rumor ran wild through the kingdom—on no account return to Jiangdu.”

sentences.zh: 後建使謁者吉請問共太后，太后泣謂吉：「歸以吾言謂而王，王前事漫漫，今當自謹，獨不聞燕齊事乎？

translations.literal: Later Jian sent his usher Ji to inquire of the Dowager of Gong; weeping, the Dowager told Ji: “Take my words back to your lord: his past conduct has been wanton—now he must be careful; has he alone not heard of what befell Yan and Qi?
translations.idiomatic: Later Liu Jian sent usher Ji to visit the Dowager of Gong; in tears she told Ji, “Go back and tell your king: his earlier misdeeds are a tangled record—now he must watch his step. Has he never heard what happened in Yan and Qi?

sentences.zh: 言吾為而王泣也。」

translations.literal: Tell him I weep for your king.”
translations.idiomatic: Go say that I weep for your king.”

sentences.zh: 吉歸，致共太后語，建大怒，擊吉，斥之。

translations.literal: When Ji returned and conveyed the Dowager of Gong’s words, Jian was greatly angry, struck Ji, and dismissed him.
translations.idiomatic: When Ji returned and repeated the dowager’s message, Jian flew into a rage, beat him, and sent him away.

sentences.zh: 建游章臺宮，令四女子乘小船，建以足蹈覆其船，四人皆溺，二人死。

translations.literal: Jian toured Zhangtai Palace, ordered four women to ride in a small boat, and Jian with his foot tipped the boat over; all four were submerged, and two died.
translations.idiomatic: At Zhangtai Palace Jian had four women board a skiff, then rocked it with his foot until it capsized; all four went under, and two drowned.

sentences.zh: 後游雷波，天大風，建使郎二人乘小船入波中。

translations.literal: Later he toured Leibo; the sky blew a great wind, and Jian ordered two gentlemen of the palace to ride a small boat into the waves.
translations.idiomatic: Later, at Leibo marsh, a gale sprang up and he sent two court gentlemen out in a little boat into the chop.

sentences.zh: 船覆，兩郎溺，攀船，乍見乍沒。

translations.literal: The boat capsized; the two gentlemen drowned, clutching the boat, now seen now submerged.
translations.idiomatic: The boat overturned; the two men clung to it, bobbing between sight and disappearance.

sentences.zh: 建臨觀大笑，令皆死。

translations.literal: Jian watched from the bank and laughed greatly, ordering that they all die.
translations.idiomatic: Jian stood on the shore laughing and ordered them left to drown.

sentences.zh: 宮人姬八子有過者，輒令臝立擊鼓，或置樹上，久者三十日乃得衣；

translations.literal: Among palace women and concubines of the eighth rank who had faults, he would order them to stand naked and beat drums, or place them in a tree; the long cases might go thirty days before they got clothing.
translations.idiomatic: Palace ladies of the eighth concubine rank who erred were made to stand naked and beat drums, or were tied up in trees; some went thirty days without clothes.

sentences.zh: 或髡鉗以鈆杵舂，不中程，輒掠；

translations.literal: Or he shaved their heads and put them in neck-irons and had them pound with lead pestles; if they did not meet the quota, they were flogged.
translations.idiomatic: Others were shorn, collared, and set to pounding with lead pestles; missing the work quota brought a beating.

sentences.zh: 或縱狼令齧殺之，建觀而大笑；

translations.literal: Or he set wolves loose to bite them to death, and Jian watched and laughed greatly.
translations.idiomatic: Sometimes he loosed wolves to tear them apart while he watched and roared with laughter.

sentences.zh: 或閉不食，令餓死。

translations.literal: Or he shut them up without food and ordered them to starve to death.
translations.idiomatic: Some he locked away without food until they starved.

sentences.zh: 凡殺不辜三十五人。

translations.literal: In all he killed thirty-five innocent persons.
translations.idiomatic: In all he murdered thirty-five innocents.

sentences.zh: 建欲令人與禽獸交而生子，彊令宮人臝而四據，與羝羊及狗交。

translations.literal: Jian wished to have people mate with beasts to produce offspring; he forced palace women to strip and spread on four corners and mate with rams and dogs.
translations.idiomatic: He wanted human–animal unions to yield children, so he stripped palace women, spread them on all fours, and forced them onto rams and dogs.

sentences.zh: 專為淫虐，自知罪多，國中多欲告言者，建恐誅，心內不安，與其后成光共使越婢下神，祝詛上。

translations.literal: He devoted himself to debauchery and cruelty; knowing his crimes were many and that many in the state wished to inform against him, Jian feared execution, was uneasy at heart, and together with his queen Cheng Guang sent Yue slave-girls to invoke spirits and curse the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Lost in lust and torture, aware of his long list of crimes and the many who would denounce him, Jian lived in terror of the axe; he and Queen Cheng Guang sent Yue bondmaids to conjure spirits and imprecate the throne.

sentences.zh: 與郎中令等語怨望：「漢廷使者即復來覆我，我決不獨死！」

translations.literal: With the superintendent of the gentlemen and others he spoke resentfully: “If Han court envoys come again to investigate me, I will certainly not die alone!”
translations.idiomatic: To his superintendent of the gentlemen and others he snarled, “The next time Han’s envoys come to rake through my affairs, I will not be the only one to die!”

sentences.zh: 建亦頗聞淮南、衡山陰謀，恐一日發，為所并，遂作兵器。

translations.literal: Jian also had heard something of the secret plots of Huainan and Hengshan; fearing that if it broke out one day he would be swallowed by them, he then made weapons.
translations.idiomatic: He had caught wind of Huainan’s and Hengshan’s conspiracy and feared being swallowed up if it erupted, so he began forging arms.

sentences.zh: 號王后父胡應為將軍。

translations.literal: He styled the queen’s father Hu Ying general.
translations.idiomatic: He gave Queen Hu’s father the title of general.

sentences.zh: 中大夫疾有材力，善騎射，號曰靈武君。

translations.literal: The grand counsellor Ji had strength of body and was skilled at riding and shooting; he was styled Lord Lingwu.
translations.idiomatic: Grand counsellor Ji, strong and expert with horse and bow, was nicknamed Lord Lingwu.

sentences.zh: 作治黃屋蓋；

translations.literal: He had a yellow imperial canopy fashioned.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered a yellow imperial canopy made.

sentences.zh: 刻皇帝璽，鑄將軍、都尉金銀印；

translations.literal: He carved an emperor’s seal and cast gold and silver seals for generals and commandants.
translations.idiomatic: He had an imperial seal carved and gold and silver seals cast for generals and commandants.

sentences.zh: 作漢使節二十，綬千餘；

translations.literal: He made twenty Han envoy credentials and more than a thousand ribbons.
translations.idiomatic: He counterfeited twenty Han-style credentials of office and over a thousand ribbon sets.

sentences.zh: 具置軍官品員，及拜爵封侯之賞；

translations.literal: He fully set out military officer grades and numbers, and rewards for investiture as marquis.
translations.idiomatic: He drew up tables of military ranks and appointments and the bounties promised for enfeoffment as marquis.

sentences.zh: 具天下之輿地及軍陳圖。

translations.literal: He fully provided maps of the empire’s territory and of army dispositions.
translations.idiomatic: He collected maps of the realm and of troop deployments.

sentences.zh: 遣人通越繇王閩侯，遺以錦帛奇珍，繇王閩侯亦遺建荃、葛、珠璣、犀甲、翠羽、蝯熊奇獸，數通使往來，約有急相助。

translations.literal: He sent men to communicate with the Yue vassal king Min Hou, presenting him brocades, silks, and rare treasures, and the vassal king Min Hou in turn presented to Jian angelica, kudzu, pearls, rhinoceros armor, kingfisher plumes, ape and bear exotica; they exchanged envoys repeatedly and agreed to aid each other in emergency.
translations.idiomatic: He dispatched agents to the Yue client king Min Hou with silks and curiosities; Min Hou replied with medicinal plants, pearls, rhinoceros-hide armor, kingfisher feathers, and rare apes and bears—envoys passed back and forth and they pledged mutual aid in crisis.

sentences.zh: 及淮南事發，治黨與，頗連及建，建使人多推金錢絕其獄。

translations.literal: When the Huainan affair broke and associates were prosecuted, Jian was somewhat implicated; Jian sent men with large sums of money to block the case.
translations.idiomatic: When the Huainan plot was exposed and accomplices were rounded up, Jian was partly entangled; he spent heavily to bury the investigation.

sentences.zh: 後復謂近臣曰：「我為王，詔獄歲至，生又無驩怡日，壯士不坐死，欲為人所不能為耳。」

translations.literal: Later he again said to close attendants: “I am a king, yet the imperial prison comes every year; in life I have not a single day of joy—a brave man does not sit waiting for death; I intend to do what others cannot do.”
translations.idiomatic: Later he told his intimates, “As a king I face imperial inquests year after year and never a day of ease—a bold man does not await death in his chair; I mean to attempt what no one else dares.”

sentences.zh: 建時佩其父所賜將軍印，載天子旗出。

translations.literal: At the time Jian wore on his belt the general’s seal his father had given him and rode out bearing the Son of Heaven’s banner.
translations.idiomatic: He then wore the general’s seal his father had bestowed and drove abroad under the imperial standard.

sentences.zh: 積數歲，事發覺，漢遣丞相長史與江都相雜案，索得兵器璽綬節反具，有司請捕誅建。

translations.literal: After several years the affair was discovered; Han sent the chief minister’s chief clerk and the chancellor of Jiangdu to investigate jointly; they searched out weapons, seals, ribbons, credentials, and gear for rebellion; the responsible officials asked that Jian be arrested and executed.
translations.idiomatic: Within a few years the plot surfaced; the court sent the chief minister’s senior clerk and the Jiangdu chancellor to conduct a joint inquest, uncovering arms, seals, ribbons, credentials, and full rebel paraphernalia; officials demanded Jian’s arrest and execution.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「與列侯吏二千石博士議。」

translations.literal: The rescript said: "Deliberate with the ranked marquises, officials at two thousand piculs, and the erudits."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied: “Take this up with the full marquises, the ministers at two thousand piculs, and the court erudits.”

sentences.zh: 議皆曰：「建失臣子道，積久，輒蒙不忍，遂謀反逆。

translations.literal: They all deliberated and said: "Jian failed the way of subject and son; for a long time he repeatedly received forbearing mercy, then plotted rebellion and treason.
translations.idiomatic: Their unanimous verdict was: “Jian betrayed every duty of a vassal and a son; again and again he was spared, yet he went on to plot open rebellion.”

sentences.zh: 所行無道，雖桀紂惡不至於此。

translations.literal: What he practiced was not the Way; even the evil of Jie and Zhou did not reach this.
translations.idiomatic: His conduct was lawless in the extreme—not even the worst tales of Jie and Zhou go this far.”

sentences.zh: 天誅所不赦，當以謀反法誅。」

translations.literal: What Heaven would punish and not pardon—he should be executed according to the law on rebellion."
translations.idiomatic: He is beyond what any pardon can cover and must die under the statutes that punish treason.”

sentences.zh: 有詔宗正、廷尉即問建。

translations.literal: An edict ordered the director of the imperial clan and the commandant of justice to interrogate Jian forthwith.
translations.idiomatic: An edict instructed the director of the imperial clan and the commandant of justice to question Jian at once.

sentences.zh: 建自殺，后成光等皆棄市。

translations.literal: Jian killed himself; Queen Cheng Guang and the others were all exposed in the marketplace.
translations.idiomatic: Jian took his own life; Queen Cheng Guang and her accomplices were executed in the public market.

sentences.zh: 六年國除，地入于漢，為廣陵郡。

translations.literal: In the sixth year the state was abolished; the territory passed into Han and became Guangling commandery.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year the kingdom was struck off; its lands reverted to the Han and were organized as Guangling commandery.

sentences.zh: 絕百二十一年，平帝時新都侯王莽秉政，興滅繼絕，立建弟盱眙侯子宮為廣陵王，奉易王後。

translations.literal: After extinction for one hundred twenty-one years, in Emperor Ping's time Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang held power, reviving extinguished lines and continuing cut-off sacrifices, and established Gong, son of the Marquis of Xuyi, Jian's younger brother, as King of Guangling, to continue Prince Yi's line.
translations.idiomatic: The line had been dead for a hundred and twenty-one years when, under Emperor Ping, Xindu marquis Wang Mang—then directing the government—revived fallen houses and named Gong, son of Jian’s younger brother the marquis of Xuyi, king of Guangling so that Prince Yi’s posterity would not lapse.

sentences.zh: 莽篡，國絕。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang seized the throne, that kingship too ended.

sentences.zh: 膠西於王端

translations.literal: Prince Yu of Jiaoxi, Duan
translations.idiomatic: Liu Duan, Prince Yu of Jiaoxi

sentences.zh: 膠西于王端，孝景前三年立。

translations.literal: Prince Yu of Jiaoxi Duan was established in the third year before Emperor Jing's accession.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Duan, Prince Yu of Jiaoxi, was ennobled in the third year of Emperor Jing’s pre-accession era.

sentences.zh: 為人賊盭，又陰痿，一近婦人，病數月。

translations.literal: In person he was cruel and perverse; moreover he suffered hidden impotence—once he approached a woman he fell ill for several months.
translations.idiomatic: He was vicious by nature and impotent besides: a single night with a woman laid him low for months.

sentences.zh: 有所愛幸少年，以為郎。

translations.literal: He had a beloved favored youth whom he appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He kept a handsome youth whom he favored and enrolled among his gentlemen attendants.

sentences.zh: 郎與後宮亂，端禽滅之，及殺其子母。

translations.literal: The gentleman consorted illicitly with the rear palace; Duan seized and exterminated him, and also killed the mother of his child.
translations.idiomatic: When that attendant took one of the harem women, Duan had him seized and killed—along with the woman and the child she had borne.

sentences.zh: 數犯法，漢公卿數請誅端，天子弗忍，而端所為滋甚。

translations.literal: He repeatedly violated the law; Han ministers repeatedly asked that Duan be executed, but the Son of Heaven could not bear it, while Duan's conduct grew worse.
translations.idiomatic: He broke the law again and again; the high ministers of Han repeatedly demanded his death, yet the emperor kept showing mercy—while Duan’s crimes only mounted.

sentences.zh: 有司比再請，削其國，去太半。

translations.literal: The responsible officials repeatedly memorialized; his state was diminished, more than half removed.
translations.idiomatic: Officials kept pressing their case until the court stripped more than half his territory.

sentences.zh: 端心慍，遂為無訾省。

translations.literal: Duan's heart was resentful; thereupon he kept no accounts and made no economies.
translations.idiomatic: Sullen at the loss, Duan simply abandoned fiscal discipline—no inventories, no economies.

sentences.zh: 府庫壞漏，盡腐財物，以鉅萬計，終不得收徙。

translations.literal: The treasuries broke and leaked; stored goods wholly rotted, amounting to tens of millions, and in the end could not be collected or moved.
translations.idiomatic: Storehouses collapsed and leaked; goods worth untold millions rotted where they lay and could neither be salvaged nor moved.

sentences.zh: 令吏毋得收租賦。

translations.literal: He ordered that clerks not be allowed to collect rent and taxes.
translations.idiomatic: He forbade his officials to levy rent or land tax.

sentences.zh: 端皆去衛，封其宮門，從一門出入。

translations.literal: Duan dismissed all guards, sealed the palace gates, and used only one gate for coming and going.
translations.idiomatic: He dismissed his household guard, walled up every palace gate but one, and came and went only through that single opening.

sentences.zh: 數變名姓，為布衣，之它國。

translations.literal: He repeatedly changed his name and surname, acted as a commoner, and went to other states.
translations.idiomatic: He often changed his name, dressed as a commoner, and slipped into neighboring kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 相二千石至者，奉漢法以治，端輒求其罪告之，亡罪者詐藥殺之。

translations.literal: When chancellors or two-thousand-picul officials arrived and acted according to Han law to govern, Duan would seek out their offenses and denounce them; those without guilt he would kill with poisoned drugs by deception.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a chancellor or a minister at two thousand piculs tried to rule by Han statute, Duan hunted for pretexts to impeach them; the innocent he murdered with poison in secret.

sentences.zh: 所以設詐究變，彊足以距諫，知足以飾非。

translations.literal: By these means he deployed deception and pursued shifts—strength enough to reject remonstrance, cleverness enough to gloss over wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Such stratagems gave him the muscle to brush off remonstrance and the wit to paper over his crimes.

sentences.zh: 相二千石從王治，則漢繩以法。

translations.literal: If chancellors and two-thousand-picul men followed the king in governing, then Han restrained them with the law.
translations.idiomatic: If those same officials instead did the king’s bidding, the central government hauled them up on legal charges.

sentences.zh: 故膠西小國，而所殺傷二千石甚眾。

translations.literal: Therefore though Jiaoxi was a small state, those of two-thousand-picul rank whom he killed or wounded were very many.
translations.idiomatic: Thus a tiny kingdom of Jiaoxi accounted for an extraordinary toll of murdered or ruined ministers at two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 立四十七年薨，無子，國除。

translations.literal: After forty-seven years of rule he died; having no son, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the forty-seventh year of his reign without an heir, and the fief was abolished.

sentences.zh: 地入于漢，為膠西郡。

translations.literal: The territory passed into Han and became Jiaoxi commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His lands were annexed to Han as Jiaoxi commandery.

sentences.zh: 趙敬肅王彭祖

translations.literal: Prince Jingsu of Zhao, Pengzu
translations.idiomatic: Liu Pengzu, Prince Jingsu of Zhao

sentences.zh: 趙敬肅王彭祖以孝景前二年立為廣川王。

translations.literal: Prince Jingsu of Zhao Pengzu was established as King of Guangchuan in the second year before Emperor Jing's accession.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Pengzu, the future Prince Jingsu of Zhao, was first made king of Guangchuan in Emperor Jing’s second pre-accession year.

sentences.zh: 趙王遂反破後，徙王趙。

translations.literal: After Prince Zhao Sui's rebellion was defeated, he was transferred to be king of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: When Prince Liu Sui of Zhao’s revolt collapsed, the court moved Pengzu onto the throne of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 彭祖為人巧佞，卑諂足共，而心刻深，好法律，持詭辯以中人。

translations.literal: Pengzu by nature was crafty and flattering, low and ingratiating enough to please, while his mind was harsh and deep; he loved statutes and precedents and wielded sophistry to strike men.
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu was sly and obsequious, eager to please on the surface yet ruthless underneath; he delighted in the code and used twisted pleading to ruin anyone who crossed him.

sentences.zh: 多內寵姬及子孫。

translations.literal: He had many favored concubines in the inner chambers and descendants.
translations.idiomatic: His inner quarters teemed with favored women and children.

sentences.zh: 相二千石欲奉漢法以治，則害於王家。

translations.literal: If chancellors or two-thousand-picul officials wished to uphold Han law in governing, it harmed the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a chancellor or a two-thousand-picul minister tried to enforce Han law, Pengzu treated it as a blow against his own house.

sentences.zh: 是以每相二千石至，彭祖衣帛布單衣，自行迎除舍，多設疑事以詐動之，得二千石失言，中忌諱，輒書之。

translations.literal: Therefore whenever a chancellor or two-thousand-picul man arrived, Pengzu put on plain unlined cloth of silk, personally went to welcome him and sweep the guest quarters, set up many doubtful matters to unsettle him by fraud; when he caught the official misspeaking and hitting taboo, he would write it down.
translations.idiomatic: So each new arrival found Pengzu waiting in plain silk, sweeping out the guest rooms himself and laying verbal traps until the man misspoke or touched a taboo—every slip went into a written record.

sentences.zh: 二千石欲治者，則以此迫劫；

translations.literal: Those who wished to govern he would with this coerce and extort;
translations.idiomatic: Officials who still meant to do their job he blackmailed with those notes;

sentences.zh: 不聽，乃上書告之，及汙以姦利事。

translations.literal: if they did not heed him, he would memorialize accusing them, moreover defaming them with venal profit matters.
translations.idiomatic: those who refused he denounced at court, smearing them with trumped-up corruption charges.

sentences.zh: 彭祖立六十餘年，相二千石無能滿二歲，輒以罪去，大者死，小者刑。

translations.literal: For more than sixty years of Pengzu's rule no chancellor or two-thousand-picul official lasted two years—always removed on some charge, the greater cases death, the lesser punishment.
translations.idiomatic: In sixty-odd years on the throne he never let a chancellor or two-thousand-picul appointee serve two full years: each was cashiered on some pretext—death in the gravest cases, mutilating sentence in the lighter ones.

sentences.zh: 以故二千石莫敢治，而趙王擅權。

translations.literal: For this reason none of two-thousand-picul rank dared govern, and the King of Zhao monopolized power.
translations.idiomatic: After that no minister dared actually govern, and the king of Zhao ruled as he pleased.

sentences.zh: 使使即縣為賈人榷會，入多於國租稅。

translations.literal: He sent envoys to the counties to act as merchant monopolists at market junctions; the income exceeded the state's rent and tax.
translations.idiomatic: He posted agents in the counties to monopolize market fees like licensed merchants; their rake-off exceeded the kingdom’s ordinary tax yield.

sentences.zh: 以是趙王家多金錢，然所賜姬諸子，亦盡之矣。

translations.literal: Therefore the house of the King of Zhao had much gold and coin, yet what he bestowed on concubines and sons was also exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: The royal treasury swelled with cash even as gifts to concubines and sons drained it dry.

sentences.zh: 彭祖不好治宮室禨祥，好為吏。

translations.literal: Pengzu did not love to build palaces or attend to auspicious omens; he loved to act as a clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu cared little for palaces or portents; what he loved was playing magistrate.

sentences.zh: 上書願督國中盜賊。

translations.literal: He memorialized asking permission to supervise bandits and robbers within the state.
translations.idiomatic: He petitioned for authority to hunt down bandits inside Zhao.

sentences.zh: 常夜從走卒行徼邯鄲中。

translations.literal: He constantly at night followed runners on patrol through Handan.
translations.idiomatic: Night after night he trailed foot soldiers on street patrol through Handan.

sentences.zh: 諸使過客，以彭祖險陂，莫敢留邯鄲。

translations.literal: Envoys and passing guests, because Pengzu was treacherous and crooked, none dared stay in Handan.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys and travelers, knowing how treacherous Pengzu was, avoided stopping in Handan altogether.

sentences.zh: 久之，太子丹與其女弟及同產姊姦。

translations.literal: After a long time, the heir Prince Dan had illicit relations with his younger sister by the same mother and his eldest sister by the same parents.
translations.idiomatic: Years later the heir Liu Dan was found to have lain with his younger full sister and his elder full sister.

sentences.zh: 江充告丹淫亂，又使人椎埋攻剽，為姦甚眾。

translations.literal: Jiang Chong denounced Dan for debauchery and disorder, moreover for having men rob graves, raid and plunder—many wicked deeds.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Chong impeached him for incest and debauchery, for hiring thugs to rob tombs and waylay travelers, and for a host of other crimes.

sentences.zh: 武帝遣使者發吏卒捕丹，下魏郡詔獄，治罪至死。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu sent envoys who mobilized clerks and soldiers to arrest Dan and committed him to the imperial prison of Wei commandery, prosecuting him to death penalty.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu sent agents with troops to seize Dan and remanded him to the imperial jail in Wei commandery on capital charges.

sentences.zh: 彭祖上書冤訟丹，願從國中勇敢擊匈奴，贖丹罪，上不許。

translations.literal: Pengzu memorialized pleading Dan's innocence, wishing to lead the brave men of his state to strike the Xiongnu and redeem Dan's crime; the emperor did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu pleaded that Dan had been wronged and offered to lead Zhao’s stalwarts against the Xiongnu to buy off the sentence; the emperor refused.

sentences.zh: 久之，竟赦出。

translations.literal: After a long time, in the end he was pardoned and released.
translations.idiomatic: In time, however, Dan was pardoned and freed.

sentences.zh: 後彭祖入朝，因帝姊平陽隆慮公主，求復立丹為太子，上不許。

translations.literal: Later when Pengzu came to court, through the emperor's elder sister the Princess Longlü of Pingyang he sought to have Dan re-established as heir; the emperor did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: Later, at court, Pengzu used the emperor’s sister Princess Longlü of Pingyang to beg that Dan be restored as heir; again the emperor said no.

sentences.zh: 彭祖取江都易王寵姬，王建所姦淖姬者，甚愛之，生一男，號淖子。

translations.literal: Pengzu took the favored concubine of Prince Yi of Jiangdu, the Nao concubine whom King Jian had debauched—he loved her greatly; she bore a male child styled the Nao lad.
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu took as consort the Lady Nao—once a favorite of Prince Yi of Jiangdu whom the depraved Liu Jian had also seduced—and doted on her; she bore a son known as “the Nao boy.”

sentences.zh: 彭祖以征和元年薨，諡敬肅王。

translations.literal: Pengzu died in the first year of Zhenghe; canonically he was Prince Jingsu.
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu died in the first Zhenghe year and received the posthumous title Prince Jingsu.

sentences.zh: 彭祖薨時，淖姬兄為漢宦者，上召問：「淖子何如？」

translations.literal: When Pengzu died, Nao concubine's elder brother was a Han eunuch; the emperor summoned and asked: "What of the Nao lad?"
translations.idiomatic: At Pengzu’s death the Lady Nao’s brother was serving as a palace eunuch; the emperor called him in and asked, “What sort of man is the Nao boy?”

sentences.zh: 對曰：「為人多欲。」

translations.literal: He replied: "In conduct he has great many desires."
translations.idiomatic: The man answered, “He is insatiably ambitious.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「多欲不宜君國子民。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: "One of great many desires is not fit to rule a state or be father to the people."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, “A man of boundless appetite is unfit to hold a kingdom or shepherd the people.”

sentences.zh: 問武始侯昌，曰：「無咎無譽。」

translations.literal: He asked Marquis of Wushi Chang, who said: "Without fault and without praise."
translations.idiomatic: He put the same question to Marquis Chang of Wushi, who replied, “Neither blame nor praise attaches to him.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「如是可矣。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: "If so, it will do."
translations.idiomatic: “That will do,” said the emperor.

sentences.zh: 遣使者立昌，是為頃王，十九年薨。

translations.literal: He sent envoys to establish Chang—this was Prince Qing—who after nineteen years died.
translations.idiomatic: He dispatched envoys to invest Chang as king—Prince Qing of Zhao—who reigned nineteen years and died.

sentences.zh: 子懷王尊嗣，五年薨。

translations.literal: His son Zun, Prince Huai, succeeded; after five years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Zun, Prince Huai, succeeded and died within five years.

sentences.zh: 無子，絕二歲。

translations.literal: Having no son, the line was cut for two years.
translations.idiomatic: He left no heir, and the succession stood vacant for two years.

sentences.zh: 宣帝立尊弟高，是為哀王，數月薨。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan established Zun's younger brother Gao—this was Prince Ai—who after several months died.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan then raised Zun’s younger brother Liu Gao to the throne as Prince Ai; he died a few months later.

sentences.zh: 子共王充嗣，五十六年薨。

translations.literal: His son Chong, Prince Gong, succeeded; after fifty-six years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Chong, Prince Gong, ruled fifty-six years before his death.

sentences.zh: 子隱嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Yin succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yin succeeded; the line died out under Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 初，武帝復以親親故，立敬肅王小子偃為平干王，是為頃王，十一年薨。

translations.literal: Initially, Emperor Wu again for kinship's sake established Yan, youngest son of Prince Jingsu, as King of Pinggan—this was Prince Qing—who after eleven years died.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, out of kindness to kin, Emperor Wu had ennobled Yan, Prince Jingsu’s youngest son, as king of Pinggan—Prince Qing—who died in the eleventh year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子繆王元嗣，二十五年薨。

translations.literal: His son Yuan, Prince Miu, succeeded; after twenty-five years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yuan, Prince Miu, held the fief twenty-five years and died.

sentences.zh: 大鴻臚禹奏：「元前以刃賊殺奴婢，子男殺謁者，為刺史所舉奏，罪名明白。

translations.literal: Grand herald Yu memorialized: "Yuan formerly with a blade murdered slave-girls and maids; his son killed an usher; he was denounced by the regional inspector—the charges are clear.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Herald Yu reported: “Yuan once hacked slave-girls to death; his son murdered an usher; a provincial inspector impeached him—the facts are plain.

sentences.zh: 病先令，令能為樂奴婢從死，迫脅自殺者凡十六人，暴虐不道。

translations.literal: In his dying instructions he ordered slave-girls who could perform music to follow him in death; those coerced to kill themselves were sixteen in all—violent cruelty violating the Way.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed he ordered every musically trained slave-girl to follow him to the grave; sixteen were driven to suicide—cruelty that shocks the moral order.

sentences.zh: 故春秋之義，誅君之子不宜立。

translations.literal: Therefore by the meaning of the Spring and Autumn, the son of a punished lord ought not to be established.
translations.idiomatic: By the Spring and Autumn Annals’ principle, the heir of a prince so condemned ought not to inherit.”

sentences.zh: 元雖未伏誅，不宜立嗣。」

translations.literal: Though Yuan was not yet executed, it is not fitting to establish a successor."
translations.idiomatic: Even though Yuan has not yet been put to death, no successor should be named.”

sentences.zh: 奏可，國除。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved the memorial and abolished the kingdom.

sentences.zh: 中山靖王勝

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Zhongshan, Sheng
translations.idiomatic: Liu Sheng, Prince Jing of Zhongshan

sentences.zh: 建元三年，代王登、長沙王發、中山王勝、濟川王明來朝，天子置酒，勝聞樂聲而泣。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianyuan, the Kings of Dai Deng, of Changsha Fa, of Zhongshan Sheng, and of Jichuan Ming came to court; the Son of Heaven set out wine; Sheng heard the music and wept.
translations.idiomatic: In the third Jianyuan year the kings of Dai (Liu Deng), Changsha (Liu Fa), Zhongshan (Liu Sheng), and Jichuan (Liu Ming) attended court; when the emperor gave a banquet and the musicians struck up, Liu Sheng began to weep.

sentences.zh: 問其故，勝對曰：

translations.literal: They asked the reason; Sheng replied, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Asked why, he answered:

sentences.zh: 臣聞悲者不可為絫欷，思者不可為歎息。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that one who grieves cannot stifle repeated sighs; one who broods cannot hold back lamenting breaths.
translations.idiomatic: “I have heard that a man in grief cannot choke back sob after sob, nor a man deep in thought hold back long sighs.”

sentences.zh: 故高漸離擊筑易水之上，荊軻為之低而不食；

translations.literal: Therefore when Gao Jianli struck the zhu on the bank of the Yi River, Jing Ke for it hung his head low and would not eat;
translations.idiomatic: When Gao Jianli plucked his lute on the banks of the Yi, Jing Ke hung his head and forgot his meal;

sentences.zh: 雍門子壹微吟，孟嘗君為之於邑。

translations.literal: when Yongmen Ziyi softly chanted, Lord Mengchang for it fell into silent grief.
translations.idiomatic: when Yongmen Ziyi hummed a few lines, Lord Mengchang sank into wordless sorrow.

sentences.zh: 今臣心結日久，每聞幼眇之聲，不知涕泣之橫集也。

translations.literal: Now your servant's heart has been knotted long; whenever I hear subtle distant music, I know not how tears gather across my face.
translations.idiomatic: My own heart has been tied in knots for years; at the merest thread of music I cannot tell how tears flood down.”

sentences.zh: 夫眾喣漂山，聚蚊成雷，朋黨執虎，十夫橈椎。

translations.literal: Now the many breathe and move mountains; gathered mosquitoes become thunder; cliques seize like tigers; ten men bend the mallet.
translations.idiomatic: “Many mouths can huff a hill into the river; swarms of gnats buzz like thunder; cabals grip like tigers; ten commoners can snap the executioner’s mallet.”

sentences.zh: 是以文王拘於牖里，孔子阨於陳、蔡。

translations.literal: For this King Wen was detained at Youli; Confucius was distressed at Chen and Cai.
translations.idiomatic: That is why King Wen lay in chains at Youli and why Confucius starved between Chen and Cai.

sentences.zh: 此乃烝庶之成風，增積之生害也。

translations.literal: This is the completed wind of the masses—the harm born of accumulation piling up.
translations.idiomatic: It is the way of the crowd once habit is set, and the injury that builds when slander piles up unchecked.”

sentences.zh: 臣身遠與寡，莫為之先，眾口鑠金，積毀銷骨，叢輕折軸，羽翮飛肉，紛驚逢羅，潸然出涕。

translations.literal: Your servant's person is distant and friendless; none walks ahead for him—many mouths melt metal; piled slander consumes bone; a cluster of light things breaks the axle; feathers fly flesh—I rush startled into nets and shed flowing tears.
translations.idiomatic: “I stand far from the capital with few friends; no one speaks for me ahead of time. Many mouths melt bronze; stacked lies gnaw bone; enough light loads will snap an axle; feathers can yet carry flesh. I stumble from panic into snares, and tears come of themselves.”

sentences.zh: 臣聞白日曬光，幽隱皆照；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that when the bright sun throws its light, hidden recesses are all illuminated;
translations.idiomatic: “I have heard that when the noon sun shines, the darkest cranny is lit;

sentences.zh: 明月曜夜，蚊虻宵見。

translations.literal: the bright moon illumines the night, gnats and midges appear by night.
translations.idiomatic: when the bright moon rules the night, midges show plain in the dark;

sentences.zh: 然雲蒸列布，杳冥晝昏；

translations.literal: Yet when clouds steam and spread in ranks, deep gloom turns day to dusk;
translations.idiomatic: yet let vapors bank and spread, and broad noon turns twilight;

sentences.zh: 塵埃抪覆，昧不泰山。

translations.literal: when dust and ashes spread cover, Mount Tai is unseen.
translations.idiomatic: let dust blot out the air, and even Mount Tai vanishes from sight.”

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: How is it?
translations.idiomatic: “Why?

sentences.zh: 物有蔽之也。

translations.literal: Things have what veils them.
translations.idiomatic: Because something stands between the eye and the truth.”

sentences.zh: 今臣雍閼不得聞，讒言之徒蹒生道遼路遠，曾莫為臣聞，臣竊自悲也。

translations.literal: Now your servant is blocked and cut off from being heard; slanderers multiply; the way is long and the road far, and none has ever spoken on your servant's behalf—your servant privately grieves.
translations.idiomatic: “Today I am shut out from the throne; calumniators breed unchecked; the capital is far away and no one has ever spoken for me—I mourn alone.”

sentences.zh: 臣聞社鼷不灌，屋鼠不熏。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the rats of the soil-altar are not flushed out, the rats of the house are not fumigated.
translations.idiomatic: “I have heard that no one flushes the rats from the soil-altar, no one smokes the rats from the roof-beams—

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why is that?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 所託者然也。

translations.literal: Because of what they rely on.
translations.idiomatic: It is because of who shelters them.

sentences.zh: 臣雖薄也，得蒙肺附；

translations.literal: Though your servant is mean, he has received to lie against the lungs and liver (imperial kin);
translations.idiomatic: “Mean though I am, I have been counted among the emperor’s own flesh;

sentences.zh: 位雖卑也，得為東藩，屬又稱兄。

translations.literal: Though his position is low, he has been able to be an eastern screen; in kinship moreover he is called elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: humble though my rank is, I hold an eastern fief and stand as elder brother in the imperial clan.”

sentences.zh: 今群臣非有葭莩之親，鴻毛之重，群居黨議，朋友相為，使夫宗室擯卻，骨肉冰釋。

translations.literal: Now the assembled ministers lack kinship thin as reed film or weight of a goose feather, yet herd together in faction and debate, friends acting for one another, causing the imperial house to be thrust aside and bone and flesh to thaw apart.
translations.idiomatic: “Yet your ministers share neither reed-thin kinship nor the weight of a goose feather with the throne; they herd in factions, scratch one another’s backs, and leave the house of Liu shoved aside while kin grow strangers.”

sentences.zh: 斯伯奇所以流離，比干所以橫分也。

translations.literal: This is why Boqi was driven to wander in exile, and why Bi Gan was cut apart at the waist.
translations.idiomatic: That is how Boqi was cast out to wander, and how Bi Gan ended on the executioner's block.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「我心憂傷，惄焉如擣；

translations.literal: The Odes says: "My heart is grieved; I am restless as if pounded;
translations.idiomatic: The Odes says: “My heart is sick; I toss as though pounded;

sentences.zh: 假寐永歎，唯憂用老；

translations.literal: dozing as if asleep I sigh endlessly; only grief makes old;
translations.idiomatic: I feign sleep yet sigh without end; care alone has aged me;

sentences.zh: 心之憂矣，疢如疾首」，臣之謂也。

translations.literal: the heart's grief is like headache pain"—this speaks of your servant.
translations.idiomatic: my heart’s ache is a fever in the skull”—that verse is mine.”

sentences.zh: 具以吏所侵聞。

translations.literal: He fully reported what the clerks had encroached upon.
translations.idiomatic: He then laid out in full every outrage local officials had committed against the kings.

sentences.zh: 於是上乃厚諸侯之禮，省有司所奏諸侯事，加親親之恩焉。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor enriched the rites toward the feudal lords, reduced what the responsible offices memorialized regarding the lords' affairs, and increased the grace of kin toward kin.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor responded by magnifying the courtesies due feudal lords, cutting back the routine denunciations ministries sent up about them, and showing the house of Liu a warmer hand.

sentences.zh: 其後更用主父偃謀，令諸侯以私恩自裂地分其子弟，而漢為定制封號，輒別屬漢郡。

translations.literal: Afterward they further employed Zhufu Yan's plan, ordering the feudal lords by private grace themselves to split land and divide it among their sons and younger brothers, while Han fixed the titles and registers, and the portions were separately attached to Han commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: The court then adopted Zhufu Yan’s scheme: lords were to carve up their domains among sons and brothers as a private favor, while the Han set the formal titles so that each new parcel fell under a Han-run commandery.

sentences.zh: 漢有厚恩，而諸侯地稍自分析弱小云。

translations.literal: Han had great grace, yet the lords' lands gradually analyzed themselves and became weak, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: The dynasty still showed them favor, but piece by piece their territories were partitioned until the kingdoms withered away.

sentences.zh: 勝為人樂酒好內，有子百二十餘人。

translations.literal: Sheng by nature loved wine and the inner chambers; he had more than a hundred twenty sons.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Sheng was fond of wine and women and fathered well over a hundred sons.

sentences.zh: 常與趙王彭祖相非曰：「兄為王，專代吏治事。

translations.literal: He constantly exchanged barbs with the King of Zhao Pengzu, saying: "As elder brother you are king yet monopolize replacing the clerks in handling affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He often needled the king of Zhao, Liu Pengzu: “You sit on a throne yet spend your days playing magistrate.”

sentences.zh: 王者當日聽音樂，御聲色。」

translations.literal: A king ought daily to listen to music and indulge sound and color."
translations.idiomatic: “A prince,” he said, “should pass his hours with music and pleasure.”

sentences.zh: 趙王亦曰：「中山王但奢淫，不佐天子拊循百姓，何以稱為藩臣！」

translations.literal: The King of Zhao also said: "The King of Zhongshan is only extravagant and dissolute; he does not assist the Son of Heaven in comforting the common people—how can he be called a screen vassal!"
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu shot back: “The king of Zhongshan does nothing but wallow in luxury and lust—he never helps the emperor console the people. What sort of bulwark is that?”

sentences.zh: 四十三年薨。

translations.literal: After forty-three years he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the forty-third year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子哀王昌嗣，一年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Ai Chang succeeded; after one year he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Chang, Prince Ai, succeeded and died within a year.

sentences.zh: 子康王昆侈嗣，二十一年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Kang Kunxi succeeded; after twenty-one years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Kunxi, Prince Kang, reigned twenty-one years and died.

sentences.zh: 子頃王輔嗣，四年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Qing Fu succeeded; after four years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Fu (posthumously Prince Qing) reigned four years and died.

sentences.zh: 子憲王福嗣，十七年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Xian Fu succeeded; after seventeen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Fu (posthumously Prince Xian) ruled seventeen years and died.

sentences.zh: 子懷王循嗣，十五年薨，無子，絕四十五歲。

translations.literal: His son Prince Huai Xun succeeded; after fifteen years he died, having no son; the line was cut for forty-five years.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Xun, Prince Huai, died in the fifteenth year without an heir, and the succession lapsed for forty-five years.

sentences.zh: 成帝鴻嘉二年復立憲王弟孫利鄉侯子雲客，是為廣德夷王。

translations.literal: In the second year of Hongjia of Emperor Cheng, the great-grandson of Prince Xian, Yunke son of the Marquis of Lixiang, was re-established—this was Prince Yi of Guangde.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Cheng’s second Hongjia year the court revived the line with Liu Yunke, great-grandson of Prince Xian and son of the marquis of Lixiang—Prince Yi of Guangde.

sentences.zh: 三年薨，無子，絕十四歲。

translations.literal: After three years he died, having no son; the line was cut for fourteen years.
translations.idiomatic: He died three years later without issue, and another fourteen years passed with no king.

sentences.zh: 哀帝復立雲客弟廣漢為廣平王。

translations.literal: Emperor Ai again established Yunke's younger brother Guanghan as King of Guangping.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ai then raised Yunke’s younger brother Guanghan as king of Guangping.

sentences.zh: 薨，無後。

translations.literal: He died; there was no successor.
translations.idiomatic: That king died too, leaving no heir.

sentences.zh: 平帝元始二年復立廣川惠王曾孫倫為廣德王，奉靖王後。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanshi of Emperor Ping, the great-great-grandson of Prince Hui of Guangchuan, Lun, was re-established as King of Guangde, to continue Prince Jing's line.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Ping’s second Yuanshi year Lun, a great-great-grandson of Prince Hui of Guangchuan, was made king of Guangde to carry on Prince Jing’s posterity.

sentences.zh: 王莽時絕。

translations.literal: Under Wang Mang the line ended.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang extinguished the house.

sentences.zh: 長沙定王發

translations.literal: Prince Ding of Changsha, Fa
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fa, Prince Ding of Changsha

sentences.zh: 長沙定王發，母唐姬，故程姬侍者。

translations.literal: Prince Ding of Changsha Fa—his mother was Lady Tang, formerly an attendant of Consort Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fa’s mother was Lady Tang, once a handmaid in Consort Cheng’s service.

sentences.zh: 景帝召程姬，程姬有所避，不願進，而飾侍者唐兒使夜進。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing summoned Consort Cheng; Cheng had something to avoid and was unwilling to attend, so she dressed her attendant Tang Er and sent her in by night.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Jing called for Consort Cheng, she was indisposed and would not go; she sent her maid Tang Er, dressed as herself, to the bedchamber that night.

sentences.zh: 上醉，不知，以為程姬而幸之，遂有身。

translations.literal: The emperor was drunk, did not know, thought it was Consort Cheng and favored her—thereupon she conceived.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, drunk, mistook the girl for Cheng and lay with her; she conceived.

sentences.zh: 已乃覺非程姬也。

translations.literal: Afterward he realized it was not Consort Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Only afterward did he discover the truth.

sentences.zh: 及生子，因名曰發。

translations.literal: When the son was born, on that account he named him Fa.
translations.idiomatic: When the boy was born he named him Fa—“sent away”—to mark the episode.

sentences.zh: 以孝景前二年立。

translations.literal: He was established in the second year before Emperor Jing's accession.
translations.idiomatic: He received his fief in the second year of Emperor Jing’s pre-accession era.

sentences.zh: 以其母微無寵，故王卑溼貧國。

translations.literal: Because his mother was lowly and without favor, therefore the kingdom was a low, damp, poor state.
translations.idiomatic: Because his mother stood low in favor, his domain was a small, marshy, impoverished kingdom.

sentences.zh: 廣川惠王越

translations.literal: Prince Hui of Guangchuan, Yue
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yue, Prince Hui of Guangchuan

sentences.zh: 後數月，下詔曰：「廣川惠王於朕為兄，朕不忍絕其宗廟，其以惠王孫去為廣川王。」

translations.literal: Several months later an edict said: "Prince Hui of Guangchuan is to Us an elder brother; We cannot bear to cut off his ancestral temple—let his grandson Qu be King of Guangchuan."
translations.idiomatic: Months later an edict ran: “Prince Hui of Guangchuan was my elder brother; I cannot let his sacrifices lapse—install his grandson Qu as king of Guangchuan.”

sentences.zh: 去即繆王齊太子也，師受易、論語、孝經皆通，好文辭方技博弈倡優。

translations.literal: Qu was the crown prince of Prince Miu Qi; in study he received the Changes, Analects, and Classic of Filial Piety—all were mastered; he loved literary phrasing, occult arts, chess, and performers.
translations.idiomatic: Qu had been heir to Prince Miu, Liu Qi; he had mastered the Changes, Analects, and Filial Piety under tutors, yet doted on belles-lettres, occult lore, board games, and actors.

sentences.zh: 其殿門有成慶畫，短衣大恊長劍，去好之，作七尺五寸劍，被服皆效焉。

translations.literal: On the palace gate was a painting of Cheng Qing—short coat, wide sleeves, long sword; Qu loved it and fashioned a sword seven chi five cun long, and his dress all imitated it.
translations.idiomatic: The gate bore a mural of the hero Cheng Qing in short jacket, billowing sleeves, and long blade; Qu copied the look, forging a seven-foot-five sword and matching his wardrobe to the picture.

sentences.zh: 有幸姬王昭平、王地餘，許以為后。

translations.literal: He had favored concubines Wang Zhaoping and Wang Diyu, whom he promised to make queen.
translations.idiomatic: He favored Wang Zhaoping and Wang Diyu and promised each the crown of chief consort.

sentences.zh: 去嘗疾，姬陽成昭信侍視甚謹，更愛之。

translations.literal: When Qu was once ill, the concubine Yangcheng Zhaoxin attended and nursed him with great care; he came to love her more.
translations.idiomatic: During an illness the concubine Yangcheng Zhaoxin nursed him devotedly, and he transferred his affection to her.

sentences.zh: 去與地餘戲，得袖中刀，笞問狀，服欲與昭平共殺昭信。

translations.literal: Qu played with Diyu, found a knife in her sleeve, flogged her and questioned the situation; she confessed that she and Zhaoping had meant together to kill Zhaoxin.
translations.idiomatic: While sporting with Diyu he found a dagger in her sleeve; under the lash she admitted she and Zhaoping had plotted Zhaoxin’s murder.

sentences.zh: 笞問昭平，不服，以鐵鍼鍼之，彊服。

translations.literal: He flogged and questioned Zhaoping; she would not confess, so with an iron needle he needled her until she was forced to confess.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoping refused to confess until he drove iron needles into her flesh.

sentences.zh: 乃會諸姬，去以劍自擊地餘，令昭信擊昭平，皆死。

translations.literal: Thereupon he assembled the concubines; Qu with his sword himself struck Diyu, ordered Zhaoxin to strike Zhaoping—both died.
translations.idiomatic: He then lined up the harem: he ran Diyu through himself and ordered Zhaoxin to kill Zhaoping.

sentences.zh: 昭信曰：「兩姬婢且泄口。」

translations.literal: Zhaoxin said: "The two concubine-maids will presently leak word."
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoxin warned, “Those two maids will talk.”

sentences.zh: 復絞殺從婢三人。

translations.literal: Again he strangled three attendant maids to death.
translations.idiomatic: He strangled three more handmaids.

sentences.zh: 後昭信病，夢見昭平等以狀告去。

translations.literal: Later Zhaoxin fell ill and dreamed that Zhaoping and the others reported the matter to Qu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhaoxin later fell ill, she dreamed the dead women were denouncing her to the king.

sentences.zh: 去曰：「虜乃復見畏我！

translations.literal: Qu said: "Those captives again appear to frighten me!"
translations.idiomatic: Qu snarled, “Those wretches still think they can haunt me!”

sentences.zh: 獨可燔燒耳。」

translations.literal: They can only be burned."
translations.idiomatic: “There is one cure—fire.”

sentences.zh: 掘出尸，皆燒為灰。

translations.literal: They dug out the corpses and all burned them to ash.
translations.idiomatic: The bodies were exhumed and burned to ash.

sentences.zh: 後去立昭信為后；

translations.literal: Later Qu established Zhaoxin as queen;
translations.idiomatic: He then raised Zhaoxin to queen;

sentences.zh: 幸姬陶望卿為脩靡夫人，主繒帛；

translations.literal: the favored concubine Tao Wangqing was made Lady of Xiumi, in charge of silks;
translations.idiomatic: he named his favorite Tao Wangqing “Lady of Xiumi” to oversee brocades and silks;

sentences.zh: 崔脩成為明貞夫人，主永巷。

translations.literal: Cui Xiucheng was made Lady of Mingzhen, in charge of the Eternal Lane.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Xiucheng became “Lady of Mingzhen,” mistress of the harem lane.

sentences.zh: 昭信復譖望卿曰：「與我無禮，衣服常鮮于我，盡取善繒饨諸宮人。」

translations.literal: Zhaoxin again slandered Wangqing, saying: "She is rude to me; her clothes are always fresher than mine; she takes all the fine silks and hoards them for the palace women."
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoxin whispered that Wangqing insulted her, dressed finer than the queen, and cornered the best silks for other women.

sentences.zh: 去曰：「若數惡望卿，不能減我愛；

translations.literal: Qu said: "Though you repeatedly malign Wangqing, you cannot lessen my love for her;
translations.idiomatic: Qu replied, “Slander her all you like—you will not cool my fondness;

sentences.zh: 設聞其淫，我亨之矣。」

translations.literal: if I hear of her lewdness, I will boil her."
translations.idiomatic: but give me proof of adultery and I will boil her alive.”

sentences.zh: 後昭信謂去曰：「前畫工畫望卿舍，望卿袒裼傅粉其傍。

translations.literal: Later Zhaoxin said to Qu: "Formerly when the painter depicted Wangqing's quarters, Wangqing stripped to the waist and put powder on beside him.
translations.idiomatic: Later Zhaoxin said, “When artists painted her rooms, Wangqing stripped to the waist and powdered herself beside them;

sentences.zh: 又數出入南戶窺郎吏，疑有姦。」

translations.literal: Moreover she repeatedly went in and out the south door peeping at the gentlemen—suspect adultery."
translations.idiomatic: she kept slipping through the south gate to spy on the gentlemen—I smell adultery.”

sentences.zh: 去曰：「善司之。」

translations.literal: Qu said: "Watch it well."
translations.idiomatic: “Keep her under watch,” said Qu.

sentences.zh: 以故益不愛望卿。

translations.literal: For this reason he increasingly did not love Wangqing.
translations.idiomatic: His love for Wangqing cooled.

sentences.zh: 後與昭信等飲，諸姬皆侍，去為望卿作歌曰：「背尊章，嫖以忽，謀屈奇，起自絕。

translations.literal: Later, drinking with Zhaoxin and the others, all concubines attending, Qu for Wangqing composed a song: "Turning from honored rule, dissipated and sudden; plots bizarre—self-cut-off.
translations.idiomatic: At a banquet with the women he sang of her: “You turned from honor, reckless and rash; your scheming was twisted—you cut your own thread;

sentences.zh: 行周流，自生患，諒非望，今誰怨！」

translations.literal: Roaming in circuits, you brought calamity on yourself; indeed it was not what was hoped—now whom do you blame!"
translations.idiomatic: you wandered in circles and brewed your own grief; this was never my wish—whom can you blame now?”

sentences.zh: 使美人相和歌之。

translations.literal: He ordered the beauties to harmonize and sing it.
translations.idiomatic: He made the concubines join in the chorus.

sentences.zh: 去曰：「

translations.literal: Qu said: "
translations.idiomatic: He added, “

sentences.zh: 是中當有自知者。」

translations.literal: Among these there ought to be those who know themselves."
translations.idiomatic: someone here knows exactly what she has done.”

sentences.zh: 昭信知去已怒，即誣言望卿歷指郎吏臥處，具知其主名，又言郎中令錦被，疑有姦。

translations.literal: Zhaoxin knew Qu was already angry; immediately she falsely said Wangqing had pointed out each gentleman's sleeping place, fully knew their masters' names, and moreover said the chief gentleman of the palace had a brocade quilt—suspect adultery.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing his rage, Zhaoxin invented a tale that Wangqing catalogued where each officer slept, knew them all by name, and shared a brocade coverlet with the chief gentleman—clear proof of intrigue.

sentences.zh: 去即與昭信從諸姬至望卿所，臝其身，更擊之。

translations.literal: Qu immediately with Zhaoxin led the concubines to Wangqing's place, stripped her body, and alternately struck her.
translations.idiomatic: He marched the harem to Wangqing’s rooms, stripped her, and beat her by turns.

sentences.zh: 令諸姬各持燒鐵共灼望卿。

translations.literal: He ordered each concubine to hold heated iron and together cauterize Wangqing.
translations.idiomatic: Each woman was handed a red-hot iron to sear her flesh.

sentences.zh: 望卿走，自投井死。

translations.literal: Wangqing ran, threw herself in a well and died.
translations.idiomatic: Wangqing broke away and drowned herself in a well.

sentences.zh: 昭信出之，椓杙其陰中，割其鼻脣，斷其舌。

translations.literal: Zhaoxin had her pulled out, drove a stake into her private parts, cut off her nose and lips, cut off her tongue.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoxin had the corpse hauled up, impaled her groin, and hacked off nose, lips, and tongue.

sentences.zh: 謂去曰：「前殺昭平，反來畏我，今欲靡爛望卿，使不能神。」

translations.literal: She said to Qu: "Formerly we killed Zhaoping and she came back to frighten me; now I want to rot Wangqing so she cannot become a spirit."
translations.idiomatic: She told Qu, “After we killed Zhaoping her ghost terrified me; now we must dissolve Wangqing so she can never haunt us.”

sentences.zh: 與去共支解，置大鑊中，取桃灰毒藥并煮之，召諸姬皆臨觀，連日夜靡盡。

translations.literal: Together with Qu they dismembered her, placed her in a great cauldron, took peach-wood ash and poison medicine and boiled them together; they summoned all concubines to watch nearby; continuously day and night until completely dissolved.
translations.idiomatic: Together they carved the body apart, dumped it into a kettle with peach ash and poison, and boiled it while the whole harem watched until nothing remained.

sentences.zh: 復共殺其女弟都。

translations.literal: Together they also killed her younger sister Du.
translations.idiomatic: They murdered Wangqing’s younger sister Du as well.

sentences.zh: 後去數召姬榮愛與飲，昭信復譖之，曰：「榮姬視瞻，意態不善，疑有私。」

translations.literal: Later Qu repeatedly summoned the concubine Rong Ai to drink; Zhaoxin again slandered her, saying: "Concubine Rong's glances and bearing are not good—suspect a private affair."
translations.idiomatic: When Qu began favoring Rong Ai, Zhaoxin whispered that the girl’s eyes and manner looked guilty of a secret lover.

sentences.zh: 時愛為去刺方領繡，去取燒之。

translations.literal: At that time Ai was embroidering a square collar for Qu; Qu took it and burned it.
translations.idiomatic: Ai was stitching a square collar for him; he snatched the work and threw it into the fire.

sentences.zh: 愛恐，自投井。

translations.literal: Ai in fear threw herself in a well.
translations.idiomatic: Terrified, she leapt into a well.

sentences.zh: 出之未死，笞問愛，自誣與醫姦。

translations.literal: They pulled her out not yet dead; flogging and questioning Ai, she falsely confessed to adultery with a physician.
translations.idiomatic: They fished her out still breathing; under torture she confessed to sleeping with a doctor— a lie wrung from her.

sentences.zh: 去縛繫柱，燒刀灼潰兩目，生割兩股，銷鈆灌其口中。

translations.literal: Qu bound her to a pillar, burned a knife and cauterized until both eyes festered, alive cut both thighs, melted lead and poured it into her mouth.
translations.idiomatic: He bound her to a post, seared out her eyes, carved living flesh from her thighs, and poured molten lead down her throat.

sentences.zh: 愛死，支解以棘埋之。

translations.literal: When Ai died, they dismembered her and buried her among brambles.
translations.idiomatic: When she died they quartered the corpse and buried it in a thorn patch.

sentences.zh: 諸幸於去者，昭信輒譖殺之，凡十四人，皆埋太后所居長壽宮中。

translations.literal: Those whom Qu favored, Zhaoxin always slandered and killed—fourteen persons in all—all buried in Changshou Palace where the queen dowager dwelt.
translations.idiomatic: Every woman who won the king’s eye Zhaoxin denounced to death—fourteen in all—interred beneath Changshou Palace, the queen dowager’s residence.

sentences.zh: 宮人畏之，莫敢復迕。

translations.literal: The palace people feared her; none dared again oppose.
translations.idiomatic: The household lived in terror of her and no one dared cross her.

sentences.zh: 昭信欲擅愛，曰：「王使明貞夫人主諸姬，淫亂難禁。

translations.literal: Zhaoxin wished to monopolize favor, saying: "The king made Lady Mingzhen oversee the concubines—debauchery is hard to forbid.
translations.idiomatic: To monopolize the king she said, “Lady Mingzhen cannot curb the harem’s whoring;

sentences.zh: 請閉諸姬舍門，無令出敖。」

translations.literal: Request to close the doors of all concubine chambers and not let them out to ramble."
translations.idiomatic: seal every concubine’s door and stop them roaming free.”

sentences.zh: 使其大婢為僕射，主永巷，盡封閉諸舍，上籥於后，非大置酒召，不得見。

translations.literal: She made her chief maid superintendent of the Eternal Lane, fully sealed and closed all chambers, and submitted the keys to the queen—except when a great wine feast was summoned, they could not be seen.
translations.idiomatic: She put her head maid in charge of the lane, locked every suite, and turned the keys over to the queen so that no woman saw the king except at his grand banquets.

sentences.zh: 去憐之，為作歌曰：「愁莫愁，居無聊。

translations.literal: Qu pitied them and for them composed a song: "Sorrow—do not sorrow—dwelling without diversion.
