translations.idiomatic: Pitying them, he wrote: “Grief need not be deepest grief—only boredom in these rooms;

sentences.zh: 心重結，意不舒。

translations.literal: The heart is heavily knotted, the mind not at ease.
translations.idiomatic: the heart knots tight, the spirit finds no ease;

sentences.zh: 內茀鬱，憂哀積。

translations.literal: Inwardly stifled, grief and sorrow pile up.
translations.idiomatic: choked within, sorrow stacks on sorrow;

sentences.zh: 上不見天，生何益！

translations.literal: Above one does not see the sky—what benefit in living!
translations.idiomatic: no sky above—what use is life?

sentences.zh: 日崔隤，時不再。

translations.literal: The sun sinks—time does not come again.
translations.idiomatic: The sun slides down—this hour will not return;

sentences.zh: 願棄軀，死無悔。」

translations.literal: I wish to cast away the body—death without regret."
translations.idiomatic: I would cast off this shell and die without regret.”

sentences.zh: 令昭信聲鼓為節，以教諸姬歌之，歌罷輒歸永巷，封門。

translations.literal: He ordered Zhaoxin to beat the drum for the beat, to teach the concubines to sing it; when the song ended they always returned to the Eternal Lane and the door was sealed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoxin drummed time while the women learned the dirge; when it ended they were marched back to the sealed harem.

sentences.zh: 獨昭信兄子初為乘華夫人，得朝夕見。

translations.literal: Only Zhaoxin's brother's son Chu, as Lady of Chenghua, could see morning and evening.
translations.idiomatic: Only Zhaoxin’s nephew Chu, styled Lady of Chenghua, still saw the king morning and night.

sentences.zh: 昭信與去從十餘奴博飲游敖。

translations.literal: Zhaoxin and Qu with more than ten slaves gambled, drank, and roamed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoxin and the king roamed with a dozen slaves, dicing and drinking.

sentences.zh: 初去年十四五，事師受易，師數諫正去，去益大，逐之。

translations.literal: Chu in the previous year was fourteen or fifteen; serving a teacher he received the Changes; the teacher repeatedly remonstrated and corrected Qu; Qu grew more extreme and expelled him.
translations.idiomatic: Chu was about fourteen when his tutor, teaching him the Changes, kept rebuking the king’s conduct; Qu grew furious and drove the man away.

sentences.zh: 內史請以為掾，師數令內史禁切王家。

translations.literal: The chancellor asked to appoint him clerk; the teacher repeatedly ordered the chancellor strictly to restrain the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor hired the tutor as clerk, and the tutor kept urging him to rein in the royal household.

sentences.zh: 去使奴殺師父子，不發覺。

translations.literal: Qu sent a slave to kill the teacher and his son; it was not discovered.
translations.idiomatic: Qu had a slave murder tutor and son; the crime went undetected.

sentences.zh: 後去數置酒，令倡俳臝戲坐中以為樂。

translations.literal: Later Qu repeatedly set out wine and ordered performers and jesters to strip and play sitting in the midst as amusement.
translations.idiomatic: He later staged feasts where actors and buffoons stripped and sported in the hall for his amusement.

sentences.zh: 相彊劾繫倡，闌入殿門，奏狀。

translations.literal: The chancellor forcibly impeached, bound the performers, burst through the palace gate, and memorialized the situation.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor seized the players for forcing the palace gate and memorialized the offense.

sentences.zh: 事下考案，倡辭，本為王教脩靡夫人望卿弟都歌舞。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down for examination; the performers' testimony said they had originally been ordered by the king to teach the Lady of Xiumi Wangqing's younger brother Du to sing and dance.
translations.idiomatic: Under interrogation the troupe said the king had ordered them to train Wangqing’s brother Du to sing and dance for the inner chambers.

sentences.zh: 使者召望卿、都，去對皆淫亂自殺。

translations.literal: Envoys summoned Wangqing and Du; Qu replied that both had been debauched and committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: When envoys called for Wangqing and Du, Qu claimed both had died by their own hand for shameful conduct.

sentences.zh: 會赦不治。

translations.literal: It met an amnesty and was not prosecuted.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty arrived and the case lapsed.

sentences.zh: 望卿前亨煮，即取他死人與都死并付其母。

translations.literal: Wangqing had previously been boiled; then he took another dead person together with Du's corpse and delivered them both to their mother.
translations.idiomatic: Wangqing was already stewed in the kettle; Qu substituted another corpse for hers and sent that body with Du’s to their mother.

sentences.zh: 母曰：「都是，望卿非也。」

translations.literal: The mother said: "That one is Du; it is not Wangqing."
translations.idiomatic: Their mother looked at the remains and said, “This corpse is Du’s; that is not my daughter Wangqing.”

sentences.zh: 數號哭求死，昭信令奴殺之。

translations.literal: She repeatedly wailed and cried seeking death; Zhaoxin ordered a slave to kill her.
translations.idiomatic: She shrieked for death until Zhaoxin had a slave cut her down.

sentences.zh: 奴得，辭服。

translations.literal: The slave was seized and confessed.
translations.idiomatic: The slave was caught and confessed.

sentences.zh: 本始三年，相內史奏狀，具言赦前所犯。

translations.literal: In the third year of Benshi, the chancellor and palace secretary memorialized the situation, fully stating what had been committed before the amnesty.
translations.idiomatic: In the third Benshi year chancellor and inner secretary laid out every crime committed before the last amnesty.

sentences.zh: 天子遣大鴻臚、丞相長史、御史丞、廷尉正雜治鉅鹿詔獄，奏請逮捕去及后昭信。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven sent the grand herald, the chief minister's chief clerk, the vice censor-in-chief, and the chief investigator of the commandant of justice jointly to try the case in the Julu imperial prison, memorializing to request arrest of Qu and Queen Zhaoxin.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor dispatched the grand herald, the chief minister’s senior clerk, the assistant imperial censor, and the senior judge of the commandant of justice to try the case at the Julu imperial jail and asked for Qu and Queen Zhaoxin to be taken into custody.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「王后昭信、諸姬奴婢證者皆下獄。」

translations.literal: The rescript said: "Queen Zhaoxin, the concubines, and slave-maids who testified—all down to prison."
translations.idiomatic: The rescript read: “Imprison Queen Zhaoxin, the concubines, and every bondmaid witness.”

sentences.zh: 辭服。

translations.literal: They confessed.
translations.idiomatic: They broke and confessed.

sentences.zh: 有司復請誅王。

translations.literal: The responsible officials again memorialized requesting execution of the king.
translations.idiomatic: Officials again asked that the king be put to death.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「與列侯、中二千石、二千石、博士議。」

translations.literal: The rescript said: "Deliberate with the full marquises, ministers at full two thousand and two thousand piculs, and the erudits."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied: “Refer the matter to the full marquises, ministers at two thousand piculs (full and regular grades), and the erudits.”

sentences.zh: 議者皆以為去悖虐，聽后昭信讒言，燔燒亨煮，生割剝人，距師之諫，殺其父子。

translations.literal: Those deliberating all held that Qu was perverse and cruel, listened to Queen Zhaoxin's slander, burned and boiled victims, alive cut and flayed people, rejected the teacher's remonstrance, and killed him and his son.
translations.idiomatic: The consensus was that Qu had turned savage, obeyed Queen Zhaoxin’s lies, roasted and boiled people alive, flayed them living, spurned his tutor’s warnings, and murdered tutor and son.

sentences.zh: 凡殺無辜十六人，至一家母子三人，逆節絕理。

translations.literal: In all he killed sixteen guiltless persons, even to one household of mother and child three persons—rebellious norm, principle cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Sixteen innocents had died, including a mother and her two children in one family—conduct that broke every human bond.

sentences.zh: 其十五人在赦前，大惡仍重，當伏顯戮以示眾。

translations.literal: Fifteen of them were before the amnesty; the great evil is still grave—they ought to suffer manifest execution to show the multitude.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen of those killings predated the amnesty; the enormity remained such that he should die in public as a warning to all.”

sentences.zh: 制曰：「朕不忍致王於法，議其罰。」

translations.literal: The rescript said: "We cannot bear to subject the king to the law; deliberate his punishment."
translations.idiomatic: A second rescript ran: “I cannot bring myself to try the king as a common felon—decide an appropriate penalty instead.”

sentences.zh: 有司請廢勿王，與妻子徙上庸。

translations.literal: The responsible officials asked to depose him from kingship and exile him with wife and children to Shangyong.
translations.idiomatic: They proposed stripping him of his title and banishing him with his family to Shangyong.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved.

sentences.zh: 與湯沐邑百戶。

translations.literal: They granted a bath fief of one hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: He was also granted a maintenance estate of a hundred households.

sentences.zh: 去道自殺，昭信棄市。

translations.literal: Qu committed suicide on the way; Zhaoxin was exposed in the marketplace.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qu killed himself en route; Queen Zhaoxin was executed in the market square.

sentences.zh: 立二十二年，國除。

translations.literal: After twenty-two years of establishment the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-two years after his accession the kingdom was struck off.

sentences.zh: 後四歲，宣帝地節四年，復立去兄文，是為戴王。

translations.literal: Four years later, in the fourth year of Dijie of Emperor Xuan, Qu's elder brother Wen was re-established—this was Prince Dai.
translations.idiomatic: Four years on, in Emperor Xuan’s fourth Dijie year, Qu’s older brother Wen was restored as Prince Dai.

sentences.zh: 文素正直，數諫王去，故上立焉，二年薨。

translations.literal: Wen had always been upright and repeatedly remonstrated King Qu; therefore the emperor established him; after two years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Wen had been honest and had often rebuked Liu Qu, which was why the court chose him; he died two years later.

sentences.zh: 子海陽嗣，十五年，坐畫屋為男女臝交接，置酒請諸父姊妺飲，令仰視畫；

translations.literal: His son Haiyang succeeded; in the fifteenth year, for painting rooms with naked men and women in sexual union, setting out wine and inviting uncles and elder and younger sisters to drink, ordering them to look up at the paintings;
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Haiyang, in his fifteenth year on the throne, was charged with decorating rooms with murals of nude couples in congress, then hosting his uncles and sisters at wine and making them stare up at the scenes;

sentences.zh: 又海陽女弟為人妻，而使與幸臣姦；

translations.literal: moreover Haiyang's younger sister was another's wife, yet he made her commit adultery with a favored minister;
translations.idiomatic: he also forced his married younger sister into the bed of a favorite courtier;

sentences.zh: 又與從弟調等謀殺一家三人，已殺。

translations.literal: moreover together with his younger cousin Diao and others he plotted to kill three persons of one household—they had already been killed.
translations.idiomatic: and he joined his cousin Diao in a plot that left three members of one family dead.

sentences.zh: 甘露四年坐廢，徙房陵，國除。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Ganlu he was deposed for his crimes, moved to Fangling, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth Ganlu year he was deposed for these offenses, removed to Fangling, and his fief extinguished.

sentences.zh: 後十五年，平帝元始二年，復立戴王弟襄隄侯子瘉為廣德王，奉惠王後，二年薨。

translations.literal: Fifteen years later, in the second year of Yuanshi of Emperor Ping, Yu, son of the Marquis of Xiangdi, younger brother of Prince Dai, was re-established as King of Guangde, to continue Prince Hui's line; after two years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen years after that, Emperor Ping’s second Yuanshi year saw Yu—son of the marquis of Xiangdi and Prince Dai’s younger brother—raised as king of Guangde to maintain Prince Hui’s sacrifices; he died two years later.

sentences.zh: 子赤嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Chi succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Chi succeeded; Wang Mang later ended the line.

sentences.zh: 膠東康王寄

translations.literal: Prince Kang of Jiaodong, Ji
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ji, Prince Kang of Jiaodong

sentences.zh: 膠東康王寄以孝景中二年立，二十八年薨。

translations.literal: Prince Kang of Jiaodong Ji was established in the second year of Emperor Jing's mid-reign; after twenty-eight years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ji was ennobled in the second year of Emperor Jing’s mid-reign era and died in the twenty-eighth year.

sentences.zh: 淮南王謀反時，寄微聞其事，私作兵車鏃矢，戰守備，備淮南之起。

translations.literal: When the King of Huainan plotted rebellion, Ji faintly heard of the affair and privately made war chariots, arrowheads, and arrows, prepared attack and defense, in readiness for Huainan's rising.
translations.idiomatic: When the king of Huainan conspired, Ji caught wind of it and secretly stockpiled chariots, bolt heads, and shafts, readying both offense and defense should Huainan rise.

sentences.zh: 及吏治淮南事，辭出之。

translations.literal: When clerks prosecuted the Huainan affair, his testimony came out.
translations.idiomatic: When officials tried the Huainan case, his involvement surfaced in the depositions.

sentences.zh: 寄於上最親，意自傷，發病而死，不敢置後。

translations.literal: Ji was closest to the emperor above; his heart wounded itself; he fell ill and died, not daring to appoint an heir.
translations.idiomatic: Ji was among the emperor’s nearest kin; shamed and broken, he sickened and died without naming a successor.

sentences.zh: 於是上聞寄有長子賢，母無寵，少子慶，母愛幸，寄常欲立之，為非次，因有過，遂無所言。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor heard that Ji had an eldest son Xian whose mother lacked favor, and a younger son Qing whose mother was loved and favored; Ji had constantly wished to establish Qing, but because it was out of order and because he had faults, in the end he said nothing.
translations.idiomatic: The throne knew Ji had left two sons: Xian by a neglected consort, and Qing by a favorite he had meant to make heir—yet irregular succession and his own guilt had kept him silent.

sentences.zh: 上憐之，立賢為膠東王，奉康王祀，而封慶為六安王，王故衡山地。

translations.literal: The emperor pitied him, established Xian as King of Jiaodong to continue Prince Kang's sacrifices, and enfeoffed Qing as King of Lu'an, ruling the former territory of Hengshan.
translations.idiomatic: Out of compassion the court made Xian king of Jiaodong to continue Prince Kang’s sacrifices and enfeoffed Qing as king of Lu’an over the old Hengshan domain.

sentences.zh: 膠東王賢立十五年薨，諡為哀王。

translations.literal: King of Jiaodong Xian after fifteen years of rule died; posthumously he was Prince Ai.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xian of Jiaodong reigned fifteen years and received the posthumous title Prince Ai.

sentences.zh: 子戴王通平嗣，二十四年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Dai Tongping succeeded; after twenty-four years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Tongping, Prince Dai, ruled twenty-four years and died.

sentences.zh: 子頃王音嗣，五十四年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Qing Yin succeeded; after fifty-four years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yin, Prince Qing, held the throne fifty-four years.

sentences.zh: 子共王授嗣，十四年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Gong Shou succeeded; after fourteen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Shou, Prince Gong, died in the fourteenth year.

sentences.zh: 子殷嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Yin succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yin succeeded; Wang Mang cut off the house.

sentences.zh: 六安共王慶立三十八年薨。

translations.literal: Prince Gong of Lu'an Qing after thirty-eight years of rule died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qing, Prince Gong of Lu’an, died in the thirty-eighth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子夷王祿嗣，十年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Yi Lu succeeded; after ten years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Lu, Prince Yi, reigned ten years.

sentences.zh: 子繆王定嗣，二十二年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Miu Ding succeeded; after twenty-two years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Ding, Prince Miu, died in the twenty-second year.

sentences.zh: 子頃王光嗣，二十七年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Qing Guang succeeded; after twenty-seven years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Guang, Prince Qing, ruled twenty-seven years.

sentences.zh: 子育嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Yu succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yu succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 清河哀王乘

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Qinghe, Cheng
translations.idiomatic: Liu Cheng, Prince Ai of Qinghe

sentences.zh: 清河哀王乘以孝景中三年立，十二年薨。

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Qinghe Cheng was established in the third year of Emperor Jing's mid-reign; after twelve years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Cheng was made prince of Qinghe in the third mid-reign year of Emperor Jing and died twelve years later.

sentences.zh: 無子，國除。

translations.literal: Having no son, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: With no heir the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 常山憲王舜

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Changshan, Shun
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shun, Prince Xian of Changshan

sentences.zh: 常山憲王舜以孝景中五年立。

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Changshan Shun was established in the fifth year of Emperor Jing's mid-reign.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shun received Changshan in the fifth mid-reign year of Emperor Jing.

sentences.zh: 舜，帝少子，驕淫，數犯禁，上常寬之。

translations.literal: Shun was the emperor's youngest son, arrogant and dissolute, repeatedly violating prohibitions; the emperor constantly treated him leniently.
translations.idiomatic: Shun was the emperor’s youngest boy—proud, wanton, and often lawless—yet the throne repeatedly indulged him.

sentences.zh: 三十三年薨，子勃嗣為王。

translations.literal: After thirty-three years he died; his son Bo succeeded as king.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the thirty-third year; his son Liu Bo succeeded.

sentences.zh: 初，憲王有不愛姬生長男梲，梲以母無寵故，亦不得幸於王。

translations.literal: Initially Prince Xian had a disfavored concubine who bore the eldest son Tui; Tui because his mother lacked favor likewise did not obtain the king's favor.
translations.idiomatic: Long before, Prince Xian’s neglected consort had borne his first son, Tui; the mother’s low standing meant the boy never won the king’s affection.

sentences.zh: 王后脩生太子勃。

translations.literal: Queen Xiu bore the heir apparent Bo.
translations.idiomatic: Queen Xiu was mother to the heir, Bo.

sentences.zh: 王內多，所幸姬生子平、子商，王后稀得幸。

translations.literal: The king had many inner favorites; favored concubines bore sons Ping and Shang; the queen rarely obtained favor.
translations.idiomatic: The harem was crowded with favorites who bore sons Ping and Shang, while the queen seldom shared the king’s bed.

sentences.zh: 及憲王疾甚，諸幸姬侍病，王后以妒媢不常在，輒歸舍。

translations.literal: When Prince Xian was gravely ill, the various favored concubines attended the sickness; the queen out of jealousy did not constantly remain, but always returned to her quarters.
translations.idiomatic: As the king lay dying, his favorites kept vigil; jealous, Queen Xiu stayed away and went home.

sentences.zh: 醫進藥，太子勃不自嘗藥，又不宿留侍疾。

translations.literal: When physicians presented medicine, the heir apparent Bo did not himself taste the medicine, nor did he stay overnight to attend the sickness.
translations.idiomatic: When doctors brought drafts, Crown Prince Bo neither tasted them first nor kept night watch at the bedside.

sentences.zh: 及王薨，王后、太子乃至。

translations.literal: Only when the king died did the queen and heir apparent arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Only after Shun died did queen and heir appear.

sentences.zh: 憲王雅不以梲為子數，不分與財物。

translations.literal: Prince Xian had always not reckoned Tui as a son in the count, and did not divide and give him goods or wealth.
translations.idiomatic: The king had never treated Tui as a true son or given him a share of the estate.

sentences.zh: 郎或說太子、王后，令分梲財，皆不聽。

translations.literal: Gentlemen of the palace some advised the heir apparent and queen to order division of property to Tui; all would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Attendants urged Bo and the queen to give Tui his portion; they refused.

sentences.zh: 太子代立，又不收恤梲。

translations.literal: When the heir apparent succeeded in his place, again he did not gather in and pity Tui.
translations.idiomatic: When Bo came to the throne he still refused to shelter Tui.

sentences.zh: 梲怨王后及太子。

translations.literal: Tui resented the queen and heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Tui nursed hatred for queen and crown prince.

sentences.zh: 漢使者視憲王喪，梲自言憲王病時，王后、太子不侍，及薨，六日出舍，太子勃私姦、飲酒、博戲、擊筑，與女子載馳，環城過市，入獄視囚。

translations.literal: When Han envoys inspected Prince Xian's funeral, Tui of himself stated that when Prince Xian was ill the queen and heir apparent did not attend; when he died, on the sixth day they left the mourning shed; the heir apparent Bo privately committed adultery, drank wine, played board games, struck the zhu, rode in a carriage with women racing, circled the city passing the market, and entered prison to view prisoners.
translations.idiomatic: Han’s inspectors came for the funeral; Tui volunteered that during the king’s illness queen and heir had deserted him, that they quit the mourning hut on the sixth day after his death, and that Bo had meanwhile fornicated, gambled, feasted, played the zhu, raced through the city with women in his chariot, and toured the jail for sport.

sentences.zh: 天子遣大行騫驗問，逮諸證者，王又匿之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven sent the grand coachman Qian to examine and question; he summoned all witnesses, and the king again hid them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent Grand Coachman Qian to investigate and call witnesses; Bo hid them away.

sentences.zh: 吏求捕，勃使人致擊笞掠，擅出漢所疑囚。

translations.literal: When clerks sought to arrest them, Bo sent men to seize, beat, and flog them, and on his own authority released Han's suspected prisoners.
translations.idiomatic: When officers hunted the witnesses, Bo had them seized and beaten and personally freed prisoners the Han court wanted.

sentences.zh: 有司請誅勃及憲王后脩。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized requesting execution of Bo and Queen Xiu of Prince Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Officials demanded death for Bo and for Queen Xiu.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「脩素無行，使梲陷之罪。

translations.literal: The emperor said: "Xiu has always lacked conduct; she let Tui trap her in crime.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, “Xiu has never been virtuous; she let Tui bait her into guilt.

sentences.zh: 勃無良師傅，不忍致誅。」

translations.literal: Bo lacks a good teacher and tutor; We cannot bear to impose execution."
translations.idiomatic: Bo lacked sound mentors; I will not put him to the sword.”

sentences.zh: 有司請廢勿王，徙王勃以家屬處房陵，上許之。

translations.literal: The responsible officials asked to depose him from kingship and not allow him to be king, and to move King Bo with household dependents to Fangling; the emperor permitted.
translations.idiomatic: They asked to strip Bo of his title and exile him with his household to Fangling; the emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 勃王數月，廢，國除。

translations.literal: Bo was king several months, was deposed, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Bo held the throne only a few months before deposition and the kingdom’s end.

sentences.zh: 月餘，天子為最親，詔有司曰：「常山憲王早夭，后妾不和，適孽誣爭，陷于不誼以滅國，朕甚閔焉。

translations.literal: After more than a month, the Son of Heaven, because he was closest in kin, ordered the responsible officials: "Prince Xian of Changshan died young; queen and concubines were not harmonious; legitimate and bastard sons slandered and contended, sinking into unrighteousness so as to extinguish the state—We greatly pity this.
translations.idiomatic: A month later, moved by close kinship, the emperor told his ministers: “Changshan’s Prince Xian died young; his wives turned on one another; heirs born of different mothers slandered each other until the fief collapsed—I grieve for that house.”

sentences.zh: 其封憲王子平三萬戶，為真定王；

translations.literal: Enfeoff Prince Xian's son Ping with thirty thousand households as King of Zhending;
translations.idiomatic: He therefore split the domain, giving thirty thousand households to Ping as king of Zhending;

sentences.zh: 子商三萬戶，為泗水王。」

translations.literal: son Shang thirty thousand households, as King of Sishui."
translations.idiomatic: and thirty thousand to Shang as king of Sishui.”

sentences.zh: 頃王平立二十五年薨。

translations.literal: Prince Qing Ping after twenty-five years of rule died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ping, Prince Qing of Zhending, died in the twenty-fifth year.

sentences.zh: 子烈王偃嗣，十八年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Lie Yan succeeded; after eighteen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yan, Prince Lie, reigned eighteen years.

sentences.zh: 子孝王由嗣，二十二年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Xiao You succeeded; after twenty-two years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu You, Prince Xiao, died in the twenty-second year.

sentences.zh: 子安王雍嗣，二十六年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince An Yong succeeded; after twenty-six years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yong, Prince An, ruled twenty-six years.

sentences.zh: 子共王普嗣，十五年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Gong Pu succeeded; after fifteen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Pu, Prince Gong, died in the fifteenth year.

sentences.zh: 子陽嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Yang succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yang succeeded; Wang Mang ended the line.

sentences.zh: 泗水思王商立十年薨。

translations.literal: Prince Si of Sishui Shang after ten years of rule died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shang, Prince Si of Sishui, died in the tenth year.

sentences.zh: 子哀王安世嗣，一年薨，無子。

translations.literal: His son Prince Ai Anshi succeeded; after one year he died, having no son.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Anshi, Prince Ai, died within a year without issue.

sentences.zh: 於是武帝憐泗水王絕，復立安世弟賀，是為戴王。

translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Wu pitied the cutting off of the King of Sishui and re-established Anshi's younger brother He—this was Prince Dai.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu, pitying the extinction of Sishui, raised Anshi’s younger brother He as Prince Dai.

sentences.zh: 立二十二年薨，有遺腹子煖，相內史不以聞。

translations.literal: After twenty-two years of rule he died; there was a posthumous son Nuan; the chancellor and palace secretary did not report it.
translations.idiomatic: He reigned twenty-two years and left a posthumous son, Nuan, whom his chancellor and inner secretary failed to report.

sentences.zh: 太后上書，昭帝閔之，抵相內史罪，立煖，是為勤王。

translations.literal: The queen dowager memorialized; Emperor Zhao pitied it, punished the chancellor and palace secretary with fines, and established Nuan—this was Prince Qin.
translations.idiomatic: The queen dowager wrote to court; Emperor Zhao took pity, punished the two officials, and installed Nuan as Prince Qin.

sentences.zh: 立三十九年薨。

translations.literal: After thirty-nine years of rule he died.
translations.idiomatic: Nuan ruled thirty-nine years and died.

sentences.zh: 子戾王駿嗣，三十一年薨。

translations.literal: His son Prince Li Jun succeeded; after thirty-one years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Jun, Prince Li, reigned thirty-one years.

sentences.zh: 子靖嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Jing succeeded; the line ended under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Jing succeeded; Wang Mang cut off the house.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：昔魯哀公有言：「寡人生於深宮之中，長於婦人之手，未嘗知憂，未嘗知懼。」

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Formerly Duke Ai of Lu had a saying: "I was born in the deep palace and grew up in women's hands; I never knew worry, I never knew fear."
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s comment: Duke Ai of Lu once said, “I was born behind palace walls and raised by women’s hands—I have never known care or fear.”

sentences.zh: 信哉斯言也！

translations.literal: How true are these words!
translations.idiomatic: How true those words ring!

sentences.zh: 雖欲不危亡，不可得已。

translations.literal: Though one wished not to face peril and extinction, it could not be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Even if he wished to avoid ruin, he could not.

sentences.zh: 是故古人以宴安為鴆毒，亡德而富貴，謂之不幸。

translations.literal: Therefore the ancients regarded complacent ease as poison like the zhēn draught; lacking virtue yet possessing wealth and honor, they called that misfortune.
translations.idiomatic: The ancients equated soft living with drinking poison; to be rich and exalted without moral worth they counted the true misfortune.

sentences.zh: 漢興，至于孝平，諸侯王以百數，率多驕淫失道。

translations.literal: From Han's rise down to Emperor Ping, the feudal kings numbered in the hundreds; for the most part many were arrogant, dissolute, and lost the Way.
translations.idiomatic: From the founding of Han to Emperor Ping there were hundreds of royal sons; most grew arrogant, dissolute, and abandoned the moral path.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: How is it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 沈溺放恣之中，居勢使然也。

translations.literal: They sank and drowned in indulgence and license; dwelling in such power made it so.
translations.idiomatic: They drowned in license because power made self-indulgence inevitable.

sentences.zh: 自凡人猶繫于習俗，而況哀公之倫乎！

translations.literal: Even ordinary men are still tied by custom—how much more the likes of Duke Ai!
translations.idiomatic: If commoners are slaves to habit, what hope had men like Duke Ai?

sentences.zh: 夫唯大雅，卓爾不群，河間獻王近之矣。

translations.literal: Only one of the utmost moral grandeur, eminent and not of the common herd—Prince Xian of Hejian came close to that.
translations.idiomatic: Only the grand integrity the Odes call daya—standing alone above the common run—found a near match in Prince Xian of Hejian.
